title: Choosing and combining backends
text: table summarizes thirteen backends. “Approx. names” total number name strings compiled backbone (accepted names plus synonyms); actual species count lower accepted species may several synonym entries pointing . things stand . WFO standard reference plant taxonomy default backend taxify. maintained World Flora Online consortium receives regular updates. backbone includes taxonomic ranks kingdom form, full synonym resolution authorship. COL GBIF cover kingdoms, differ curation strategy. COL expert-curated checklist assembled 160 sector databases, maintained taxonomic authority group. GBIF’s backbone assembled algorithmically COL, ITIS, dozens sources, gives broader raw coverage (~10M names vs COL’s ~4.5M) cost occasional inconsistencies source databases disagree. practice, COL tends give cleaner synonym resolution; GBIF tends match names. ITIS originally developed North American fauna remains particularly strong freshwater invertebrates, insects, US-listed species. coverage non-American taxa uneven. ITIS backbone distributed SQLite dump; building source requires RSQLite package. Pre-built .vtr backbones avoid dependency. NCBI Taxonomy gold standard sequence-linked work. Every GenBank, RefSeq, BOLD sequence linked NCBI tax_id, making backend essential molecular ecology metagenomics. also backend covers bacteria, archaea, viruses meaningful depth. However, NCBI Taxonomy store authorship data, authorship column always NA NCBI-matched rows. OTT (Open Tree Life) synthetic taxonomy merges NCBI, GBIF, WoRMS, IRMNG, several sources single tree. broadest coverage single source provides cross-references constituent databases via sourceinfo field. trade-synthetic taxonomies can carry conflicts inconsistencies edges, source databases disagree placement taxon. WoRMS authoritative source marine species. curated network 300 taxonomic editors covers marine, brackish, freshwater species. Beyond basic taxonomy, WoRMS backbone stores habitat flags (marine, brackish, freshwater, terrestrial) extinction status, accessible via COL SpeciesProfile. Euro+Med PlantBase taxonomic reference flora Europe, Mediterranean, Caucasus. covers native introduced vascular plants geographic scope (~49k accepted names, ~83k synonyms). backbone built 2020 bulk download, updated via PESI API delta refresh (April 2026) resolved 1,014 reclassifications synonym changes cross-referenced WFO POWO. Euro+Med particularly useful European vegetation surveys datasets aligned European Vegetation Archive (EVA). data licensed CC -SA 3.0. Species Fungorum Plus specialist reference fungal taxonomy, ~500k names curated Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew. covers Ascomycota, Basidiomycota, fungal phyla, including anamorphs teleomorphs. purely mycological datasets, gives better synonym resolution generalist databases. AlgaeBase covers micro- macroalgae, cyanobacteria, protists. backend licensed CC -NC (non-commercial use ). backends open-access. taxify prints license notice AlgaeBase download make visible. FishBase SeaLifeBase standard references aquatic animals. FishBase covers fishes; SeaLifeBase covers non-fish marine aquatic groups (molluscs, crustaceans, marine mammals, rest). compiled rfishbase package rather file download, taking accepted species load_taxa() synonym links synonyms(). carry species-rank rows , genera enter genus register derived species. data CC -NC 3.0. Reptile Database taxonomic reference reptiles, ~50k names covering snakes, lizards, turtles, crocodilians, tuatara. built reptarium bulk exports: current accepted-species list, periodic synonym snapshot, family--order checklist. source carries kingdom class field, backbone stamps Animalia / Chordata / Reptilia every row. data CC 4.0.

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: taxify auto-downloads backbones first use. call taxify(names, backend = "wfo") local WFO backbone exists, taxify fetches pre-built .vtr file Zenodo, writes taxify_data_dir(), caches path remainder R session. Subsequent calls, whether session future sessions, reuse local copy without network access. download backbone ahead time (useful shared server Docker image), use taxify_download_vtr(): Pre-built .vtr files hosted Zenodo typically range 50 MB (AlgaeBase) 400 MB (GBIF), depending backend. files compiled raw Darwin Core sources precomputed matching keys, embedded synonym resolution, genus-level indexes, ready querying moment download completes. taxify checks backbone updates per R session. first taxify() call session fetches manifest GitHub, compares requested backend’s local version latest available release, downloads new version one exists. network unavailable, taxify falls back bundled manifest uses whatever local copy disk. version check logged console silent updates. backends large source files, build--source path also exists. taxify_download("gbif") downloads raw 1.5 GB simple.txt.gz GBIF, parses 30 positional columns, denormalizes family hierarchy via self-joins, compiles result .vtr format. slower downloading pre-built file produces output. build--source path mainly useful CI pipelines users want customize compilation step. can also pin specific backbone version: Pinned versions stored directory (taxify_data_dir()/wfo/2024.01/) never overwritten future updates. “latest” slot (taxify_data_dir()/wfo/latest/) always overwritten newer version becomes available. Pinning useful reproducibility: project can lock specific backbone version produce identical results regardless analysis re-run.
code: library(taxify)  # Download one backbone taxify_download_vtr("wfo")  # Download several at once taxify_download_vtr(c("wfo", "col", "worms")) taxify_download_vtr("wfo", version = "2024.01")

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: simplest use case: match plant names WFO. Every row output 16 columns regardless backend produced : input_name, matched_name, accepted_name, taxon_id, accepted_id, rank, family, genus, epithet, authorship, is_synonym, is_hybrid, match_type, fuzzy_dist, backend, backbone_version. backend column records "wfo" matched rows NA unmatched ones. backbone_version column records backend name, version, download date (e.g., "wfo:2024-12 (2026-04-01)") can cite exact data snapshot used. name matches synonym, taxify automatically resolves accepted name. matched_name column shows name string actually matched backbone (may synonym), accepted_name shows current accepted name resolution. is_synonym column TRUE resolved synonyms, FALSE direct matches. Fuzzy matching default normalized Damerau-Levenshtein threshold 0.2, roughly one edit per five characters. catches common typos like transposed letters missing diacritics. can tighten threshold stricter matching: disable fuzzy matching entirely accept exact case-insensitive matches: Two alternative distance metrics available via fuzzy_method argument: "levenshtein" (standard Levenshtein, transposition handling) "jw" (Jaro-Winkler, better names differ mainly beginnings). default "dl" (Damerau-Levenshtein) good -rounder.
code: library(taxify)  plants <- c(   "Quercus robur",   "Quercus petraea",   "Pinus sylvestris",   "Acer pseudoplatanus",   "Betula pendula",   "Fagus sylvatica",   "Picea abies" )  result <- taxify(plants, backend = "wfo") result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "family", "match_type", "backend")] result <- taxify(plants, backend = "wfo", fuzzy_threshold = 0.1) result <- taxify(plants, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = FALSE)

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: thirteen backends produce 16-column output schema. deliberate design choice: downstream code need know backend produced match. said, content columns varies ways worth knowing . Authorship. WFO’s scientificName already canonical (authorship appended), authorship column comes separate scientificNameAuthorship field. COL WoRMS store full scientificName authorship included; taxify strips build time produce canonical name used matching, stripped authorship stored separately. NCBI OTT authorship data , authorship column always NA backends. GBIF ITIS provide authorship. Euro+Med provides authorship AuthorString field. Species Fungorum AlgaeBase provide authorship DwC-archives. Taxon IDs. backend uses different identifier system. WFO IDs look like "wfo-0000000123". COL IDs opaque alphanumeric strings like "4LHBG". GBIF uses integer keys ("2878688"). ITIS uses TSN integers ("183671"). NCBI uses NCBI Taxonomy IDs ("9606"). OTT uses OTT IDs ("770315"). WoRMS uses AphiaIDs extracted LSIDs; build time, taxify strips urn:lsid:marinespecies.org:taxname: prefix stores just numeric ID. Euro+Med uses TaxonUsageID integers PlantBase export. Species Fungorum AlgaeBase use ChecklistBank dataset-specific IDs. IDs stored character strings taxon_id accepted_id columns consistency, format backend-specific meaningful within backend’s ecosystem. taxon_id WFO looked COL database, vice versa. Classification depth. base output always includes family genus. WFO provides directly classification file. COL stores full Linnaean hierarchy (kingdom order) Taxon.tsv, though extra columns require add_col_info() access. GBIF provides family denormalized family_key self-join build time. ITIS, NCBI, OTT resolve family genus via parent-hierarchy walks backbone compilation; walk traverses 25 levels taxonomic tree. WoRMS denormalized classification columns directly DwC-. Euro+Med resolves family genus via hierarchy walk IsChildTaxonOfID. genus register (covered later vignette) fills higher classification fields (kingdom_group, taxon_group, life_form) backends. Synonym handling. backends represent synonymy different ways internally. WFO COL use Darwin Core field acceptedNameUsageID point synonym row accepted name. GBIF encodes synonyms via parent_key pointing accepted taxon. NCBI represents synonyms alternative name strings tax_id; build, taxify emits separate rows synthetic IDs form "123456_syn_1", "123456_syn_2", etc. OTT uses separate synonyms.tsv file explicit synonym--accepted mappings. representations normalized build time is_synonym + accepted_name + accepted_id schema, output looks regardless source. Synonym chains. backbones contain chained synonyms, synonym points synonym B, points accepted name C. taxify resolves chains build time (10 hops) accepted_name always points terminal accepted name. happens transparently backbone compilation affect query-time performance.

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: species list spans multiple kingdoms, single backend may cover everything. wetland monitoring dataset might contain vascular plants, invertebrates, amphibians, algae, fungi. single backend covers equally well. taxify’s fallback chain handles : pass vector backend names, names matched backend order. name matched earlier backend never re-matched later one; removed pool. console output matching shows chain action: "Quercus robur" matches WFO removed pool. remaining four names go COL. still unmatched COL (perhaps obscure bacterial name), go GBIF. process continues names tried backends names matched. order backends vector matters. determines taxonomic opinion wins name. "Quercus robur" exists WFO COL, putting WFO first means WFO’s taxonomic opinion used (accepted name, family assignment, synonym resolution). Putting COL first give COL’s opinion. names exist multiple backends, first backend chain always wins. practical consequences. put GBIF first, everything match (GBIF ~10M names, largest backend) curated opinions WFO, COL, WoRMS never consulted. plant-heavy list non-plant taxa mixed , c("wfo", "col") c("wfo", "col", "gbif") sensible ordering: get WFO’s curated plant taxonomy plants, COL GBIF picks rest. names matched earlier backends, later backends skipped entirely message: Fuzzy matching runs independently within backend chain. name fails exact matching WFO gets fuzzy-matched WFO. still fails, moves next backend gets exact-matched, fuzzy-matched . means misspelled plant name best chance matching WFO (plant-specialist backend) falling COL GBIF.
code: mixed <- c(   "Quercus robur",       # plant   "Panthera leo",        # animal   "Amanita muscaria",    # fungus   "Salmo trutta",        # fish   "Escherichia coli"     # bacterium )  result <- taxify(mixed, backend = c("wfo", "col", "gbif")) result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "match_type", "backend")] Matching 5 names against 3 backends: wfo -> col -> gbif   [wfo] Matching 5 names...   [col] Matching 4 remaining names...   [gbif] Matching 1 remaining names... [gbif] Skipped (all names matched)

what: Worked example: plants-only with WFO vs WFO + COL
title: Choosing and combining backends
text: WFO focuses accepted vascular plant bryophyte names. coverage excellent current taxonomy, names appear older literature, belong genera yet integrated WFO, nomenclaturally orphaned (clear accepted name) may absent. COL inherits WFO’s plant taxonomy one sector databases supplements names sources, including historical synonyms cultivar names. names come back "none", can add COL fallback: backend column now shows "wfo" names matched WFO "col" names COL resolve. tells us exactly match came , matters reproducibility. paper’s methods section, can state “plant names resolved WFO 2024-12, unmatched names resolved COL 2025.” two-backend chain especially valuable large vegetation plot datasets. names resolve WFO plant-optimized taxonomy, handful edge cases (cultivars, historical names, genera recently moved families) fall COL otherwise require manual resolution.
code: plants <- c(   "Quercus robur",   "Quercus petraea",   "Pinus sylvestris",   "Acer pseudoplatanus",   "Coffea arabica",   "Welwitschia mirabilis",   "Lepidodendron aculeatum",   # extinct lycopsid   "Nothofagus cunninghamii",   "Dracaena draco" )  # WFO alone wfo_result <- taxify(plants, backend = "wfo") table(wfo_result$match_type) # WFO first, COL as fallback both_result <- taxify(plants, backend = c("wfo", "col")) table(both_result$match_type) both_result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "backend")]

what: Worked example: mixed-kingdom list with COL + GBIF + WoRMS
title: Choosing and combining backends
text: ecological monitoring dataset coastal estuary might contain vascular plants, invertebrates, fish, marine algae. single backend covers well. COL broad expert-curated coverage across kingdoms. GBIF fills gaps larger name pool. WoRMS provides authoritative backstop marine invertebrate synonymy, can slow propagate generalist databases. COL good first choice covers kingdoms expert curation. names resolve . GBIF catches anything COL might miss, including names national checklists yet incorporated COL. WoRMS serves final backstop specifically marine taxa, covering invertebrate synonyms may lag generalist databases. chain ordered highest curation broadest coverage. list predominantly marine terrestrial taxa, might lead WoRMS instead ensure authoritative marine taxonomy takes precedence:
code: estuary_species <- c(   "Zostera marina",              # seagrass (plant)   "Salicornia europaea",         # glasswort (plant)   "Carcinus maenas",             # shore crab   "Mytilus edulis",              # blue mussel   "Platichthys flesus",          # European flounder   "Nereis diversicolor",         # ragworm   "Fucus vesiculosus",           # bladderwrack (brown alga)   "Littorina littorea",          # common periwinkle   "Arenicola marina",            # lugworm   "Cerastoderma edule"           # common cockle )  result <- taxify(estuary_species, backend = c("col", "gbif", "worms")) result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "family", "backend")] result <- taxify(estuary_species, backend = c("worms", "col"))

what: Worked example: fungi with Species Fungorum + COL fallback
title: Choosing and combining backends
text: Mycological datasets benefit using Species Fungorum Plus primary backend. curated specifically fungi, ~500k names including anamorphs, teleomorphs, pleomorphic naming changes introduced 2011 Melbourne Code. Synonym coverage fungal genera better generalist databases, fungal taxonomy often secondary concern. Species Fungorum resolves standard names. obscure, recently described, historically orphaned species fall , COL picks . mixed datasets include fungi plants, three-backend chain works well: WFO handles plants, Species Fungorum handles fungi, COL serves catch-anything falls specialist backends. genus register (see ) helps taxify skip backends possibly match given name. taxify encounters "Amanita muscaria" genus Amanita WFO’s coverage table, name marked --scope WFO immediately passed next backend without wasting time fuzzy matching.
code: fungi <- c(   "Amanita muscaria",   "Boletus edulis",   "Cantharellus cibarius",   "Tuber melanosporum",   "Saccharomyces cerevisiae",   "Aspergillus niger",   "Penicillium chrysogenum",   "Agaricus bisporus",   "Trametes versicolor",   "Cordyceps militaris" )  result <- taxify(fungi, backend = c("fungorum", "col")) result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "is_synonym", "backend")] mixed <- c(   "Quercus robur",              # plant   "Amanita muscaria",           # fungus   "Lactarius deliciosus",       # fungus   "Pinus sylvestris",           # plant   "Russula emetica"             # fungus )  result <- taxify(mixed, backend = c("wfo", "fungorum", "col"))

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: algal taxonomy, AlgaeBase specialist source. covers micro- macroalgae, cyanobacteria, protists. curation particularly strong freshwater marine microalgae generalist databases often thin coverage outdated synonymy. AlgaeBase licensed CC -NC. taxify prints license notice download users aware restriction incorporating data workflow. commercial applications, COL WoRMS can serve alternatives, though less specialized algal coverage.
code: algae <- c(   "Chlamydomonas reinhardtii",   "Chlorella vulgaris",   "Ulva lactuca",   "Fucus vesiculosus",   "Sargassum muticum" )  result <- taxify(algae, backend = c("algaebase", "col")) result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "backend")]

what: Worked example: molecular ecology with NCBI
title: Choosing and combining backends
text: reconciling species lists metabarcoding eDNA studies, names often linked NCBI accessions. Using NCBI backend ensures taxify’s accepted names align taxonomy used GenBank BOLD. taxon_id values NCBI-matched rows NCBI tax_ids, can used directly link back GenBank records NCBI taxonomy pages. names found NCBI (e.g., taxa without sequenced representatives), COL provides fallback.
code: edna_hits <- c(   "Salmo trutta",   "Phoxinus phoxinus",   "Anguilla anguilla",   "Cottus gobio",   "Lampetra planeri",   "Chironomus riparius",     # midge (insect)   "Potamopyrgus antipodarum" # New Zealand mud snail )  result <- taxify(edna_hits, backend = c("ncbi", "col")) result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "taxon_id", "backend")]

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: backend column taxify’s output plain character column. single-backend calls, every matched row shows backend name. multi-backend chains, column records backend produced match. Unmatched rows backend = NA. can use column count many names backend resolved: filter rows matched specific backend: useful quality control. expected purely plant-based list see many names matched COL instead WFO, tells us list contains names outside WFO’s scope (perhaps algae classified plants older literature, animal-associated organisms like plant parasites). backbone_version column gives full provenance string row, combining backend name, version, download date. paper’s methods section, can extract unique versions used: provenance strings identify taxonomic source exact snapshot used, making results fully reproducible even backend releases new version run analysis reviewer checks .
code: result <- taxify(species_list, backend = c("wfo", "col", "gbif")) table(result$backend, useNA = "ifany") wfo_matches <- result[result$backend == "wfo" & !is.na(result$backend), ] col_matches <- result[result$backend == "col" & !is.na(result$backend), ] unique(result$backbone_version[!is.na(result$backbone_version)]) # e.g., c("wfo:2024-12 (2026-04-01)", "col:2025 (2026-04-01)")

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: Three backends dedicated enrichment functions join additional backend-specific columns taxify result. functions enrich rows matched corresponding backend; rows backends get NA new columns.

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: Adds WFO-specific columns: scientificNameID, parentNameUsageID, namePublishedIn, higherClassification, taxonRemarks, infraspecificEpithet. namePublishedIn field particularly useful citing original descriptions, higherClassification provides full taxonomic hierarchy semicolon-separated string.
code: result <- taxify(plants, backend = "wfo") |>   add_wfo_info()  result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "namePublishedIn")]

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: Adds COL classification columns (kingdom, phylum, col_class, order), nomenclatural metadata (notho, nomenclaturalCode, nomenclaturalStatus, namePublishedIn), infraspecificEpithet, SpeciesProfile flags (is_extinct, is_marine, is_freshwater, is_terrestrial). class column renamed col_class avoid conflict R’s class() function. SpeciesProfile flags come separate file COL DwC-archive. especially useful filtering: can, instance, exclude extinct species contemporary biodiversity analysis, separate marine terrestrial taxa estuarine dataset.
code: result <- taxify(species_list, backend = "col") |>   add_col_info()  # Check which species are marine result[result$is_marine == TRUE & !is.na(result$is_marine),        c("input_name", "accepted_name", "kingdom", "is_marine")]

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: Adds GBIF-specific columns: notho_type (hybrid type), nom_status (nomenclatural status), bracket_authorship (basionym author), bracket_year, gbif_year, name_published_in, origin (name entered GBIF backbone), infra_specific_epithet. origin field useful understanding provenance: values like "SOURCE", "DENORMED_CLASSIFICATION", "VERBATIM_ACCEPTED" indicate GBIF ingested name.
code: result <- taxify(species_list, backend = "gbif") |>   add_gbif_info()  result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "origin", "nom_status")]

what: Combining extras in a multi-backend result
title: Choosing and combining backends
text: multi-backend result, can pipe multiple enrichment functions. one touches rows backend; others left alone. produces wide data.frame union extra columns. given row, columns backend populated; rest NA. Whether useful depends analysis. workflows, base 16 columns sufficient, backend-specific extras needed require nomenclatural details, habitat flags, publication references standard output include.
code: result <- taxify(species_list, backend = c("wfo", "col", "gbif")) |>   add_wfo_info() |>   add_col_info() |>   add_gbif_info()

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: taxify ships unified genus register built union genera across eight pre-built backends (WFO, COL, GBIF, ITIS, NCBI, OTT, WoRMS, Euro+Med). register contains ~100k genera, family, higher classification (kingdom order, available), life_form label (e.g., "vascular plant", "animal", "fungus"). classification resolved priority: WoRMS > COL > GBIF > Euro+Med > ITIS > NCBI > OTT > WFO. COL WFO disagree family genus belongs , COL’s assignment wins. register serves two purposes taxify’s matching pipeline. First, provides life_form, kingdom_group, taxon_group columns taxify output every matched name, regardless backend matched . columns make possible stratify results broad taxonomic group without needing look family manually. Second, register enables --scope detection. fuzzy matching begins, taxify checks whether unmatched name’s genus register. genus known (appears register) covered requested backends (appear backend coverage table), taxify marks name "out_of_scope" immediately. avoids wasting time fuzzy matching backend never produce match, gives user informative signal plain "none".

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: lookup_genus() returns register row single genus. register loaded memory first call cached session.
code: lookup_genus("Quercus") #   genus   kingdom phylum class   order   family   life_form # 1 Quercus Plantae ...    ...     Fagales Fagaceae vascular plant lookup_genus("Panthera") #   genus    kingdom  phylum   class    order     family  life_form # 1 Panthera Animalia Chordata Mammalia Carnivora Felidae animal

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: taxify_register_coverage() shows backends contain given genus version. useful diagnosing match failures: genus appear coverage table requested backend, name genuinely scope backend. genus covered three backends can matched . genus covered GBIF (perhaps recently described bacterial genus) match WFO COL.
code: taxify_register_coverage("Quercus") #     genus   backend version date_added # 1 Quercus  col     2025    2026-04-01 # 2 Quercus  gbif    current 2026-04-01 # 3 Quercus  wfo     2024-12 2026-04-01

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: taxify encounters unmatched name whose genus appears register covered requested backends, sets match_type = "out_of_scope" instead "none". distinction carries information: --scope result means name likely exists different backend rather misspelling invalid name. life_form column tells us genus belongs animals, confirming WFO wrong backend name. pipeline, can filter "out_of_scope" rows re-run broader backend. , practically, include right backends fallback chain start, --scope mechanism saves time skipping fuzzy matching step names possibly match. taxify result’s print() method includes tally --scope names broken taxon_group, making easy see glance kinds organisms outside requested backend’s scope.
code: # Trying to match a marine invertebrate against WFO (plants only) result <- taxify("Carcinus maenas", backend = "wfo") result$match_type # [1] "out_of_scope"  result$life_form # [1] "animal"

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: right backend depends taxonomic scope data. decision tree organized common use cases. Pure vascular plant lists. Use backend = "wfo". WFO standard reference, well-curated, updated regularly. names fall (e.g., horticultural cultivars, nomenclaturally complex genera, names older floras use outdated synonymy), add COL: backend = c("wfo", "col"). European vegetation data. Use backend = c("euromed", "wfo"). Euro+Med PlantBase taxonomic reference used EVA covers native introduced vascular plants Europe, Mediterranean, Caucasus. Leading Euro+Med ensures European synonym resolution follows Euro+Med’s taxonomic opinion, WFO fallback non-European taxa names outside Euro+Med’s scope. Note Euro+Med data CC -SA 3.0. Pure marine/aquatic lists. Use backend = "worms". WoRMS authoritative source marine taxonomy, curated domain experts, includes habitat extinction flags. estuarine transitional lists include terrestrial taxa, add COL: backend = c("worms", "col"). Pure fungal lists. Use backend = "fungorum". Species Fungorum Plus specialist reference fungi. Add COL fallback obscure recently described species: backend = c("fungorum", "col"). Pure algal lists. Use backend = "algaebase". Add COL WoRMS fallback: backend = c("algaebase", "col"). Remember AlgaeBase CC -NC. Mixed-kingdom ecological datasets. Use backend = c("col", "gbif"), lead specialist backend dominant taxon group. plant-dominated dataset animals fungi: backend = c("wfo", "col"). marine biodiversity survey: backend = c("worms", "col", "gbif"). forest inventory includes trees, fungi, insects, epiphytes: backend = c("wfo", "fungorum", "col"). Molecular/sequence-linked work. Use backend = "ncbi". NCBI Taxonomy reference GenBank, BOLD, sequence databases. covers bacteria, archaea, viruses backends lack. mixed molecular ecological work: backend = c("ncbi", "col"). Phylogenetic studies. Use backend = "ott". OTT backbone Open Tree Life merges multiple source taxonomies. cross-references NCBI, GBIF, WoRMS, IRMNG make good bridge different identifier systems. Maximum coverage / catch-. Use backend = c("col", "gbif"). COL provides expert-curated taxonomy ~4.5M names. GBIF’s backbone adds ~10M names additional sources. Together cover virtually described species nomenclatural record. combination reasonable default taxonomic composition dataset unknown. General rule. Specialist backends first, generalist backends second. Lead backend whose taxonomic opinion trust dominant taxon group, add broader backends fallbacks remainder. backend column output lets us audit exactly taxonomic opinion applied name.

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: Backend size affects download time , lesser extent, matching speed. WFO (~400k names) matches faster GBIF (~10M names) query. However, taxify uses index-accelerated genus-blocked joins C level (via vectra), even GBIF matching fast typical species lists. list 5,000 names resolves GBIF second modern hardware. performance difference becomes noticeable scale (100k+ names) heavy fuzzy matching large backbone. large lists multi-backend chain, putting likely backend first saves time. Names matched first backend skip later backends entirely. 90% list plants, c("wfo", "col") faster c("col", "wfo") WFO smaller resolves names first pass. remaining 10% names go COL, larger processes small residual. Fuzzy matching expensive step. runs genus-blocked fuzzy join multi-threaded string distance computation. names misspelled genera (genus blocking help), taxify falls back 2-character prefix block catches genus-level typos keeping search space manageable.

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: backbone_version column encodes exact data snapshot used. published analysis, recommend recording strings methods section supplementary material. taxify pins backbone version download time update mid-session. Version checks happen per R session, update logged console old new version numbers. lock specific backbone version project: Pinned versions live directories never overwritten. “latest” slot continues track new releases independently. project needs exact reproducibility can pin backends specific versions never use “latest” slot. project prefers stay current can rely default “latest” behavior cite backbone_version strings output. manifest, maps backend names download URLs latest versions, cached per session can refreshed taxify_refresh_manifest(). offline use, taxify falls back bundled manifest shipped package.
code: taxify_download_vtr("wfo", version = "2024.01")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: Taxonomic name matching rarely last step. taxify() resolves species list accepted names IDs, next task usually attach trait data, occurrence records, measurement tables external sources. trouble external datasets almost never use names backbone. CSV leaf trait measurements might record Pinus nigra subsp. laricio, backbone stores accepted name Pinus nigra. colleague’s spreadsheet might list Picea excelsa (synonym retired decades ago), WFO recognises Picea abies. Joining raw species strings misses cases: rows match, merged data.frame NAs values exist. standard workaround run external names backbone first, resolve accepted IDs, join IDs. add_data() wraps entire workflow single pipe step.
code: library(taxify)

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: common case: trait measurements data.frame sitting R session, want attach taxify() result. create small table specific leaf area (SLA) maximum height five European tree species. add_data() takes names taxon column, runs backbone(s) used original taxify() call, resolves accepted_id, left-joins ID. Picea excelsa synonym Picea abies WFO, SLA height values land correct row even though literal strings differ. output two new columns, sla max_height_m, appended existing result.
code: # Our taxify result species <- c(   "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Picea abies",   "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula" ) result <- taxify(species, backend = "wfo")  # External trait data — note one synonym and one subspecies traits <- data.frame(   taxon = c(     "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Picea excelsa",     "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula"   ),   sla = c(18.2, 24.1, 6.5, 8.0, 22.3),   max_height_m = c(35, 40, 50, 30, 25) )  # Join — "Picea excelsa" resolves to "Picea abies" through the backbone result <- result |> add_data(traits, species_col = "taxon")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: data lives file rather memory, can pass path directly. add_data() reads .csv .csv.gz files via vectra’s CSV reader, handles large files efficiently without loading everything R . Tab-separated files (.tsv, .tsv.gz) also supported. CSV single obvious species-name column, auto-detection picks . several plausible character columns, names encoded column unusual name like latin_binomial, specifying species_col avoids ambiguity. pattern works compressed CSV files. .csv.gz path detected decompressed transparently.
code: result <- taxify(species, backend = "wfo") result <- result |> add_data("path/to/leaf_traits.csv") result |> add_data("leaf_traits.csv", species_col = "latin_binomial") result |> add_data("global_leaf_traits.csv.gz", species_col = "species")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: Spreadsheets common ecology, especially hand-curated trait databases shared among collaborators. add_data() reads .xlsx files via openxlsx2 package, must installed separately. sheet, start_row, species_col left defaults, add_data() scans workbook find right combination automatically. tests sheet 20 candidate header rows, probing character columns backbone finds species names. handles common case colleague’s spreadsheet title block, column descriptions, notes actual data table. scan reports found: skip auto-detection, specify combination sheet, start_row, species_col explicitly:
code: # install.packages("openxlsx2")  # if not already installed result |> add_data("bird_morphometry.xlsx") Scanning Excel layout...   Detected: sheet 'measurements', header row 3, species column 'latin_name' (90% match rate) # Specific sheet by name or number result |> add_data("bird_morphometry.xlsx", sheet = "measurements") result |> add_data("bird_morphometry.xlsx", sheet = 2)  # Known header row (e.g., rows 1-2 are title/notes) result |> add_data("bird_morphometry.xlsx", start_row = 3)  # All three specified — no scanning at all result |> add_data("bird_morphometry.xlsx", sheet = 1, start_row = 3,                    species_col = "latin_name")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: trait data lives SQLite database, add_data() reads table via vectra’s SQLite reader (depends DBI RSQLite hood). single .sqlite .db file can hold many tables, table argument mandatory . Omitting raises informative error rather guessing. particularly handy already maintain relational database measurements across projects. can point add_data() relevant table without exporting CSV first, backbone matching still runs way format.
code: # SQLite — requires DBI and RSQLite result |> add_data(   "traits.sqlite",   table = "plant_traits",   species_col = "species" )

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: pre-built .vtr files (columnar format taxify uses internally backbone storage), can passed directly. vectra reads near-zero overhead parsing type inference needed. mainly useful sharing processed trait tables team members across projects. .vtr file produced one workflow can re-used another without converting back CSV. export_data() function makes easy: export_data() also supports .csv, .tsv, .xlsx interoperability tools outside R.
code: result |> add_data("prebuilt_traits.vtr", species_col = "canonical_name") # Save a taxify result (with enrichments) as .vtr result |> export_data("processed_traits.vtr")  # A colleague can load it directly other_result |> add_data("processed_traits.vtr") result |> export_data("for_excel_users.xlsx") result |> export_data("for_python.csv")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: Tab-separated files work way CSV. add_data() reads .tsv .tsv.gz files via read.delim().
code: result |> add_data("leaf_traits.tsv", species_col = "species") result |> add_data("leaf_traits.tsv.gz")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: formats directly supported (.parquet, .rds), reading file data.frame first passing add_data() works every case.
code: my_data <- readRDS("legacy_traits.rds") result |> add_data(my_data, species_col = "sp")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: species_col specified, add_data() probes character column external data. takes first 10 rows column, runs taxify() backbone, picks column highest match rate. column needs least 50% probe names match qualifies. Auto-detection works well species column contains clean binomial names character columns contain obviously non-taxonomic strings (site codes, habitat descriptions, observer names). can fail column names ambiguous, species names heavily misspelled use common names. situations, specifying species_col explicitly saves time avoids confusing error message.
code: # Auto-detection in action traits <- data.frame(   site = c("A", "A", "B", "B"),   species = c("Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica",               "Betula pendula", "Picea abies"),   habitat = c("forest", "forest", "forest edge", "boreal"),   sla = c(18.2, 24.1, 22.3, 6.5) )  # Three character columns: site, species, habitat # Only "species" will produce >50% backbone matches result |> add_data(traits)

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: default, add_data() joins columns external data except species column. external dataset dozens columns need two three, cols argument keeps output tidy. also helps columns external data create name collisions (discussed ) rather avoid entirely.
code: # Full trait table with many columns big_traits <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica"),   sla = c(18.2, 24.1),   max_height_m = c(35, 40),   leaf_nitrogen = c(2.1, 2.4),   wood_density = c(0.56, 0.58),   seed_mass_mg = c(3500, 220),   bark_thickness_mm = c(25, 8) )  # Only join SLA and wood density result |> add_data(big_traits, species_col = "species",                    cols = c("sla", "wood_density"))

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: external data columns name columns already present taxify() result, add_data() prefixes incoming columns data_. Existing columns taxify result remain unchanged regardless external data contains. message prints collisions detected, listing renamed columns. know advance collision occur need conflicting column, filtering via cols cleaner letting rename happen.
code: # The taxify result already has a "family" column # External data also has a "family" column (taxonomic family from a # different source) plus a "leaf_area" column external <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica"),   family = c("Fagaceae", "Fagaceae"),   leaf_area = c(45.2, 38.7) )  result |> add_data(external, species_col = "species") # Output gains "data_family" (from external) and "leaf_area" (no collision)

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: External datasets sometimes contain species . happens repeated measurements across sites, multiple literature sources compiled one table, subspecies resolve accepted species. add_data() distinguishes two cases. Exact duplicates occur trait values given species identical across repeated rows. harmless: duplicates collapsed single row warning. Conflicting duplicates occur species appears different trait values. “Conflicting” means least one selected trait columns differs two rows share accepted_id. comparison column--column treats two NA values equal (missing counts agreement). two rows Quercus robur SLA values 18.2 21.5, conflict. rows SLA 18.2 one NA wood density 0.56, also conflict. every selected column matches exactly across rows given species consider duplicates identical. add_data() decide value correct conflict exists, raises error names offending species. fix depends data. duplicates represent within-species variation (e.g., measurements different populations), aggregating joining right approach. represent data entry errors, removing bad rows resolves issue. cols argument can also help columns conflict: selecting non-conflicting subset lets join proceed. Note duplicates checked backbone resolution, raw names. external data lists Picea excelsa Picea abies different SLA values, two names resolve accepted species trigger conflicting-duplicate error. intentional: join key accepted ID, conflicting values key coexist.
code: # Harmless: same species, same values (perhaps from two sites) dup_ok <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica"),   sla = c(18.2, 18.2, 24.1) ) result |> add_data(dup_ok, species_col = "species") # Warning: 1 duplicate rows ... deduplicated. # Conflicting: same species, different SLA values dup_bad <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica"),   sla = c(18.2, 21.5, 24.1) ) result |> add_data(dup_bad, species_col = "species") # Error: 1 species resolved to the same accepted_id but have #   different trait values. #   Examples: 'Quercus robur' (wfo-0000309171) # Aggregate first, then join library(stats) dup_agg <- aggregate(sla ~ species, data = dup_bad, FUN = mean) result |> add_data(dup_agg, species_col = "species")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: full pipeline inside add_data() five steps: Read external data. File paths dispatched extension (.csv, .csv.gz, .tsv, .tsv.gz, .xlsx, .sqlite, .vtr). Data.frames pass directly. format detection based solely file extension, misnamed file (e.g., tab-separated file saved .csv) produce read error rather silent misparse. Identify species column, either explicit species_col argument via auto-detection. auto-detecting, add_data() samples first 10 rows every character column runs sample taxify() backbone(s). column whose sample achieves highest match rate wins, provided clears 50% threshold. Columns containing site codes, habitat labels, observer names rarely match backbone entry, true species column tends stand clearly. Match species names backbone(s) used original taxify() call. produces accepted_id row external data. backbone choice read taxify_meta attribute taxify() attaches output, need specify . Fuzzy matching default (controlled via fuzzy fuzzy_threshold). names fail resolve dropped joinable pool, count appears summary message end. Check duplicates. backbone resolution, rows share accepted_id inspected. Exact duplicates (identical trait values across selected columns) collapsed warning. Conflicting duplicates raise error, described section . Left join accepted_id. Every row original taxify() result matching accepted_id external data receives trait columns. Rows without match get NA. Column name collisions resolved prefixing incoming columns data_. join preserves every row original result; nothing dropped. Species present external data absent original result ignored. summary message reports many species matched many names external data resolved backbone.

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: default, add_data() uses fuzzy matching taxify() resolve names external data. Fuzzy matching catches typos minor spelling differences, can produce false matches short similar names. fuzzy_threshold argument controls permissive matching . Lower values stricter. Disabling fuzzy matching entirely (fuzzy = FALSE) useful external data already well-curated want avoid risk cross-species contamination approximate string matches.
code: # Strict: only very close matches result |> add_data(traits, species_col = "taxon", fuzzy_threshold = 0.1)  # Exact matching only (no fuzzy) result |> add_data(traits, species_col = "taxon", fuzzy = FALSE)

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: add_data() fits naturally pipe chain alongside built-enrichment functions. Custom data pre-built enrichments use accepted_id join key, can stacked order. step appends columns result. final data.frame contains core taxify() output, IUCN conservation status, woodiness classification, custom SLA height measurements, aligned accepted species identity.
code: result <- taxify(species, backend = "wfo") |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_woodiness() |>   add_data(traits, species_col = "taxon")

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Every add_*() function performs underlying operation: left join accepted_name column taxify() result canonical_name column enrichment .vtr file. join key accepted (resolved) name rather original input, synonyms resolved matching contribute automatically. input contained “Pinus abies” backbone resolved “Picea abies”, enrichment join looks “Picea abies” trait database. means never worry whether species list uses currently accepted names outdated synonyms: backbone resolution step already normalized everything. design deliberate consequence: enrichments produce values rows successfully matched taxify(). Rows accepted_name NA (unmatched names) always receive NA enrichment columns. species resolved backbone, looked trait database either. usually correct behavior, means improving match rates upstream (cleaning names, trying different backbone, enabling fuzzy matching) directly improves enrichment coverage downstream.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: call enrichment function, taxify executes following steps: Ensures enrichment .vtr file present disk (downloading needed). Extracts unique accepted_name values result. Writes unique names temporary .vtr file. Performs vectra inner_join() temporary names enrichment .vtr name column. Uses vectorized match() fill new trait columns back original result. Rows without match receive NA. operation fast reduces single hash-based lookup per unique accepted name, per row. result 50,000 rows 8,000 unique accepted names 8,000 lookups. vectra join exploits hash indexes canonical_name column enrichment .vtr, making even enrichments hundreds thousands rows resolve second.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: subtle important design decision underlies enrichment .vtr files: built work taxify’s seven backends (WFO, COL, GBIF, ITIS, NCBI, OTT, WoRMS). Different backbones sometimes accept different names taxon. WFO might accept “Senecio jacobaea” COL accepts “Jacobaea vulgaris” species. enrichment .vtr contained one names, fail match results backbone. taxifydb build pipeline solves resolving every source species name seven backends separately (fallback chain, return first match). union unique accepted_name values collected per source species. source row expanded: one enrichment row per distinct accepted name, trait data duplicated. final .vtr deduplicated canonical_name (plus group column grouped enrichments). practice, backends agree 90% names, expansion modest (typically 1.1–1.5x original row count). result add_conservation_status() works identically whether upstream taxify() call used WFO, COL, GBIF. pick enrichments based backbone used, worry backbone-specific name variants falling cracks.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: first time enrichment requested session, taxify checks whether local copy exists disk. , downloads pre-built .vtr GitHub Releases using URL recorded package manifest (inst/manifest.json). meta.json sidecar file written alongside .vtr, recording version string, whether dataset static, download date. subsequent calls within R session, file path served -memory cache (package-level environment), disk even touched. Across sessions, -disk copy reused without network request. enrichments marked “static” manifest (version-locked datasets like Zanne et al. 2014 PanTHERIA), version checks skipped entirely. datasets fixed, published versions never change. non-static enrichments (IUCN Red List, GRIIS, WCVP, common names), taxify performs lightweight version check per session comparing local meta.json version manifest’s latest field. newer version available, downloaded automatically console message. check adds negligible latency manifest cached.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: pre-built .vtr download fails (network issues, mirror outage, transient server errors), taxify stop immediately. Instead, attempts build enrichment original source data. 18 enrichments build recipe internal registry (.enrichment_build_registry) knows download raw CSV, ZIP, API response upstream source, parse data.frame canonical_name column, produce .vtr file locally. build--source path slower (download parse raw data rather pre-built binary), means enrichments remain available even GitHub Releases mirror temporarily . build--source also fails (e.g., upstream source unreachable), taxify falls back one level “emergency fallback”: downloads parses source data memory without writing disk, performs join -memory using data.frame rather .vtr, issues warning explaining situation. emergency result ephemeral cached. three paths fail, error raised link GitHub issue tracker failure can reported.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: enrichment .vtr files live single root directory, organized enrichment name version: taxify_data_dir() function returns platform-appropriate data directory (typically ~/.local/share/taxify Linux/macOS %LOCALAPPDATA%/taxify Windows). directory also backbone .vtr files stored, single taxify_data_dir() call reveals taxify data lives system. Enrichment files modest size: 1 20 MB. full set 18 enrichments totals roughly 150-200 MB.
code: taxify_data_dir()/   enrichment/     conservation_status/       latest/         conservation_status.vtr         meta.json     griis/       latest/         griis.vtr         meta.json     woodiness/       latest/         woodiness.vtr         meta.json     ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: applying enrichments, may want see available. list_enrichments() queries taxify manifest returns data.frame summarizing every available enrichment layer. returned columns : name, version, nrow (approximate row count), static (whether dataset version-locked), trait_cols (comma-separated list trait column names), source_url (upstream data source). static column worth paying attention . Static enrichments (woodiness, PanTHERIA, AmphiBIO, EltonTraits, LEDA, Diaz traits, FungalTraits, FUNGuild, AlgaeTraits, FISHMORPH, ReptTraits, LepTraits, AnimalTraits, NW European Arthropods) based published, version-locked datasets single definitive release. never trigger version checks, add zero network overhead session. Non-static enrichments (conservation_status, GRIIS, WCVP, common_names) periodically updated upstream source publishes new releases. , taxify checks per session whether newer version available updates transparently . nrow column gives rough sense enrichment size. Conservation status ~166,000 rows (one per assessed species), WCVP ~340,000 (one per species-region combination), smaller enrichments like LEDA ~8,000. numbers include cross-backbone name expansion discussed earlier, slightly larger original source row counts. trait_cols column lists columns enrichment adds result. useful planning enrichments apply: need specific leaf area data, scanning trait_cols column reveals LEDA provides sla_mm2_mg. need diet composition data, trait_cols elton_traits lists 18 diet, foraging, mass, nocturnality columns. source_url column points original data source (Zenodo, Figshare, Dryad, GBIF, etc.) reference citation.
code: library(taxify)  list_enrichments() #>              name version   nrow static                              trait_cols ... #> 1 conservation_status  ...  166000   TRUE                   conservation_status ... #> 2               griis  ...   23000  FALSE                      invasive_status ... #> 3                wcvp  ...  340000  FALSE                        native_status ... #> ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: workflows run computing clusters, Docker containers, environment without reliable internet access, can pre-download enrichments analysis begins. taxify_download_enrichment() function accepts character vector enrichment names downloads one local data directory. step, subsequent add_*() calls enrichments use local copies without network access. particularly useful reproducible pipelines: pre-downloading enrichments setup time guarantees analysis always uses version dataset, regardless whether newer versions published meantime. download function prints confirmation message version file size enrichment. enrichment already present requested version, re-downloaded. .vtr files live taxify_data_dir()/enrichment/{name}/latest/ alongside meta.json sidecar. Pre-downloading also useful teaching workshop settings many participants share slow network connection. One person can download enrichments, copy taxify_data_dir() contents shared drive USB stick, distribute participants. Since enrichment lookup path starts -disk check, copied files found immediately without network access.
code: # Download a single enrichment taxify_download_enrichment("conservation_status")  # Download several at once taxify_download_enrichment(c("woodiness", "eive", "leda"))  # Download all of them taxify_download_enrichment(c(   "conservation_status", "griis", "wcvp", "eive",   "elton_traits", "avonet", "pantheria", "amphibio",   "common_names", "woodiness", "diaz_traits", "leda",   "fungal_traits", "funguild", "algae_traits",   "fish_traits", "fishbase", "repttraits", "anage", "glonaf",   "leptraits", "animaltraits", "arthropod_traits", "alien_first_records",   "baseflor", "ecoflora", "floraweb" ))

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Simple enrichments add one columns via flat join accepted_name. Eighteen twenty-two enrichment layers use pattern. differ columns add taxonomic groups cover. group taxon focus, starting plants (enrichment layers), conservation status (cross-taxon), birds, mammals, amphibians, vertebrates, fungi, algae, fish, reptiles, butterflies, arthropods.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Plants best-served taxon group enrichment system, dedicated layers covering growth form, ecological niches, seed height traits, broad suite functional traits, regional trait compilations British, French, German floras. reflects state published plant trait databases: decades investment standardized trait measurement protocols produced several large, open-access datasets straightforward integrate.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: woodiness enrichment classifies ~50,000 plant species woody, herbaceous, variable. dataset comes Zanne et al. (2014), landmark study radiation angiosperms freezing environments, published Nature. underlying classification draws world’s major herbarium botanical databases. three possible values "woody", "herbaceous", "variable". "variable" category applies species exhibit growth forms depending environmental conditions ecotype. Coverage strongest angiosperms (monocots dicots) weaker ferns, lycophytes, bryophytes. dataset static (CC0 license, published 2014), never triggers version checks. Woodiness coarse trait, one widely used community ecology macroecology. separates plant strategies along fundamental axis (persistent woody stems vs. annual perennial herbaceous growth), making valuable community-weighted mean analyses, functional diversity indices, biome classification.
code: plants <- taxify(c(   "Quercus robur", "Betula pendula", "Arrhenatherum elatius",   "Festuca rubra", "Salix caprea", "Cornus sanguinea" ))  plants |> add_woodiness() #>               input_name       accepted_name woodiness #> 1          Quercus robur       Quercus robur     woody #> 2        Betula pendula      Betula pendula     woody #> 3 Arrhenatherum elatius Arrhenatherum elatius herbaceous #> 4         Festuca rubra        Festuca rubra herbaceous #> 5         Salix caprea        Salix caprea     woody #> 6    Cornus sanguinea     Cornus sanguinea     woody

what: EIVE ecological indicator values (Dengler et al. 2023)
title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: EIVE 1.0 provides continuous ecological indicator values ~14,500 European vascular plant species. supersedes classic ordinal Ellenberg indicator values, expert-assigned integers 1-9 scale, statistically derived continuous scores based species co-occurrence patterns across thousands vegetation plots. Five niche axes covered: light, temperature, moisture, soil reaction (pH), nutrients. EIVE restricted European flora, species continents receive NA five columns. continuous values scale comparable original Ellenberg system (roughly 1-9) allow fractional positions. matters community-weighted mean (CWM) calculations: averaging ordinal Ellenberg values treats intervals categories equal (difference 3 4 7 8), guaranteed. EIVE’s continuous scale makes CWM calculations statistically cleaner. five output columns prefixed eive_ avoid collision columns sources: eive_light, eive_temperature, eive_moisture, eive_reaction, eive_nutrients. EIVE dataset licensed CC 4.0 published Zenodo. classified static taxify manifest version (1.0) fixed publication. reference Dengler et al. (2023), Vegetation Classification Survey 4:7-29.
code: grasses <- taxify(c(   "Arrhenatherum elatius", "Bromus erectus", "Festuca rubra",   "Dactylis glomerata", "Lolium perenne", "Poa pratensis" ))  grasses |> add_eive() #>          input_name eive_light eive_temperature eive_moisture eive_reaction eive_nutrients #> 1 Arrhenatherum ...       7.2              5.8           4.3           7.1            6.5 #> 2    Bromus erectus       7.6              5.5           3.1           7.8            3.2 #> ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Diaz enrichment provides two key functional traits TRY database consortium: seed mass milligrams plant height metres. species-level means compiled thousands individual measurements across multiple primary sources. Coverage spans ~46,000 plant species globally, making one broader trait datasets available plants. Seed mass plant height sit two important axes global spectrum plant form function described Diaz et al. (2016, Nature 529:167-171). Seed mass captures offspring size / offspring number trade-(small-seeded species produce many propagules, large-seeded species invest fewer, better-provisioned offspring). Plant height captures competitive strategy axis (tall species intercept light invest structural tissue). Combining two traits EIVE LEDA columns produces reasonably complete functional characterization European temperate species. output columns seed_mass_mg plant_height_m. numeric (NA_real_ missing values). dataset licensed CC 3.0 distributed via TRY File Archive.
code: trees <- taxify(c(   "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Picea abies",   "Pinus sylvestris", "Acer pseudoplatanus" ))  trees |> add_diaz_traits() #>          input_name seed_mass_mg plant_height_m #> 1     Quercus robur      3200.0           25.0 #> 2  Fagus sylvatica      2200.0           30.0 #> 3      Picea abies        7.9           40.0 #> 4 Pinus sylvestris        6.5           25.0 #> 5 Acer pseudoplatanus   120.0           25.0

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: LEDA covers ~8,000 NW European plant species 10 trait columns spanning life form, dispersal, seed, leaf, clonality dimensions. column-rich simple enrichments, providing broad functional profile single call. full column set includes: raunkiaer_life_form: primary Raunkiaer life form (phanerophyte, chamaephyte, hemicryptophyte, geophyte, therophyte, helophyte, hydrophyte). raunkiaer_variable: 1 species assigned multiple life forms, 0 otherwise. dispersal_type: primary dispersal vector (anemochory, zoochory, hydrochory, autochory, barochory, dysochory). terminal_velocity_ms: seed terminal velocity m/s (species median). seed_mass_mg: seed mass mg (species median). canopy_height_m: canopy height metres (species median). leaf_mass_mg: leaf dry mass mg (species median). sla_mm2_mg: specific leaf area mm^2/mg (species median). clonal_growth: capable clonal growth (1 = yes, 0 = ). buoyancy: seed buoyancy classification. Raunkiaer life form column classifies plants perennating buds sit unfavorable season. Phanerophytes (trees, tall shrubs) hold buds 25 cm soil; chamaephytes (low shrubs, cushion plants) keep near surface. Hemicryptophytes, dominant group temperate grasslands, position buds right soil level. ground, geophytes store buds bulbs rhizomes, therophytes skip problem entirely surviving seeds. LEDA provides classification species level NW European flora, making one trait databases includes Raunkiaer assignments several thousand species. One column-name collision aware : LEDA’s seed_mass_mg Diaz enrichment’s seed_mass_mg share output column name. enrichments stacked pipe chain, second one run overwrite first. values may differ slightly LEDA reports species median measurements Diaz reports TRY consortium mean. keep , apply one enrichment, rename column, apply second. Alternatively, choose whichever source appropriate study: LEDA NW European analyses (regional measurements), Diaz global analyses (worldwide compilation).
code: meadow_spp <- taxify(c(   "Arrhenatherum elatius", "Trifolium pratense",   "Leucanthemum vulgare", "Plantago lanceolata",   "Achillea millefolium", "Centaurea jacea" ))  meadow_spp |> add_leda() #>          input_name raunkiaer_life_form dispersal_type sla_mm2_mg canopy_height_m ... #> 1 Arrhenatherum ...  hemicryptophyte     anemochory       25.1            0.90  ... #> 2 Trifolium pratense hemicryptophyte     zoochory         22.3            0.30  ... #> ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Three regional databases add trait detail European floras cover. carries region suffix every column (_uk Britain, _de Germany, Baseflor’s unsuffixed French set), can chained without clobbering one another pan-European layers . Baseflor (Julve, Programme Catminat), via add_baseflor(): 8,500 taxa French neighbouring flora, flowering months, pollination vector, dispersal mode, breeding system, flower colour, fruit type, woody growth form, continentality salinity axes absent EIVE. Ecoflora (Fitter & Peat 1994), via add_ecoflora(): British Isles flora, canopy height, leaf traits, life form, flowering phenology, pollination, seed weight, British-calibrated Ellenberg values (18 _uk columns). FloraWeb (BfN; BiolFlor data Klotz, Kuehn & Durka 2002), via add_floraweb(): German flora, morphology, reproductive biology, nine Ellenberg indicator values, ploidy chromosome number, Grime CSR strategy type, chorological distribution (59 _de columns). every column region-suffixed, one chain can attach British, French, German trait sets side side species. FloraWeb Ecoflora bundled pre-built datasets work offline; German British trait values reported published, access date dataset version (neither portal offers versioned bulk export). Italian Ellenberg-type indicator values also available add_pignatti(), reads copy bundled TR8 package demand; values come copyrighted publication redistributed taxify.
code: taxify(c("Bellis perennis", "Achillea millefolium", "Calluna vulgaris")) |>   add_ecoflora() |>   add_floraweb()

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: vascular plants form symbiosis root fungi, type symbiosis one informative functional traits plant carries: governs plant acquires nutrients soil fungi depends . add_fungalroot() attaches mycorrhizal type FungalRoot, global compilation 36,000 plant--site observations published GBIF. Unlike enrichments , FungalRoot joins genus, accepted_name. Mycorrhizal type conserved genus level (resolution FungalRoot recommends inference), value per-genus majority consensus every species covered genus inherits , whether exact binomial observed. Three columns added: mycorrhizal_type: genus-level consensus type. (arbuscular, far common, formed herbs many trees), EcM (ecto, typical oaks, pines, birches, temperate forest trees), ErM (ericoid, confined Ericaceae), OM (orchid), NM (non-mycorrhizal, e.g. Brassicaceae many Cyperaceae), dual types EcM-/ ErM-EcM / ErM-, plus uncertain. mycorrhizal_status: coarse roll-type, one "mycorrhizal", "non-mycorrhizal", "uncertain". mycorrhizal_records: many FungalRoot observations support genus-level consensus, one-record genus can told apart well-sampled one. join genus, plant whose genus FungalRoot returns NA, genus circumscribed differently across backbones may line . Coverage plant genera (4,000 genera). dataset distributed CC -NC 4.0; per-genus consensus computed taxify per-observation labels, FungalRoot’s published genus assignment.
code: taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris", "Trifolium pratense",          "Vaccinium myrtillus", "Brassica oleracea")) |>   add_fungalroot() #>          input_name     genus mycorrhizal_type mycorrhizal_status mycorrhizal_records #> 1       Quercus robur   Quercus              EcM        mycorrhizal                 163 #> 2    Pinus sylvestris     Pinus              EcM        mycorrhizal                 500 #> 3  Trifolium pratense Trifolium               AM        mycorrhizal                 193 #> 4 Vaccinium myrtillus Vaccinium              ErM        mycorrhizal                 227 #> 5   Brassica oleracea  Brassica               NM    non-mycorrhizal                  59

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: conservation status enrichment enrichment spans taxonomic groups equally. Coverage includes ~166,000 species assessed IUCN Red List, representation across plants, vertebrates, invertebrates, fungi. single column added: conservation_status, containing standard IUCN category code. seven categories order increasing threat : LC (Least Concern): population stable, significant threats. NT (Near Threatened): close qualifying threatened category. VU (Vulnerable): facing high risk extinction wild. EN (Endangered): facing high risk extinction. CR (Critically Endangered): facing extremely high risk. EW (Extinct Wild): survives captivity cultivation. EX (Extinct): known living individuals. Species yet assessed IUCN receive NA. IUCN assessed nearly mammals, birds, amphibians, reptiles, fraction invertebrates, fungi, plants. plant-focused study, coverage rates likely lower (perhaps 10-30% species list) vertebrate study (90-100% coverage typical). enrichment also includes species assessed DD (Data Deficient), indicates IUCN examined species lacks sufficient data assign threat category. conservation status enrichment non-static: IUCN publishes updated assessments several times per year, taxify enrichment rebuilt new assessments become available. summary() output show version string (e.g., “2025.1”) exact assessment vintage can cited.
code: species <- taxify(c(   "Panthera tigris", "Ailuropoda melanoleuca",   "Gorilla gorilla", "Vulpes vulpes",   "Passer domesticus", "Quercus robur" ))  species |> add_conservation_status() #>            input_name conservation_status #> 1     Panthera tigris                  EN #> 2 Ailuropoda melanoleuca               VU #> 3      Gorilla gorilla                  CR #> 4        Vulpes vulpes                  LC #> 5   Passer domesticus                  LC #> 6       Quercus robur                  LC

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Birds served two complementary enrichments together provide detailed functional ecological profile. AVONET covers morphology migration strategy; EltonTraits covers diet composition foraging behavior. intentional overlap body mass (provide ), remaining columns distinct.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: AVONET provides species-level morphological measurements ~11,000 bird species worldwide, based direct measurements museum specimens live birds. enrichment adds 11 columns covering beak dimensions (length, depth), wing length, tail length, tarsus length, body mass, hand-wing index, primary habitat, trophic level, trophic niche, migration strategy. morphological measurements (beak, wing, tail, tarsus) millimetres, representing species means across measured specimens. hand-wing index (hand_wing_index) quantifies wing pointedness correlates strongly long-distance flight ability: swifts, falcons, shearwaters score high, wrens, rails, pheasants sit low end spectrum. Dispersal ecology, macroecology, studies range expansion make heavy use index. migration column three possible values: "sedentary" (non- migratory), "partial" (populations migrate), "full" (obligate long-distance migrant). trophic_niche column uses categories like "Invertivore", "Omnivore", "Vertivore", "Frugivore", "Granivore", "Nectarivore", "Herbivore aquatic", others. AVONET licensed CC 4.0 published Figshare. reference Tobias et al. (2022), Ecology Letters 25:581-597. dataset classified static taxify manifest.
code: birds <- taxify(c(   "Parus major", "Cyanistes caeruleus", "Erithacus rubecula",   "Turdus merula", "Falco peregrinus", "Aquila chrysaetos" ))  birds |> add_avonet() #>        input_name beak_length wing_length avonet_body_mass_g migration trophic_niche ... #> 1     Parus major        11.2        75.1               18.5 sedentary  Invertivore  ... #> 2 Cyanistes caeruleus    9.8        67.2               11.0 sedentary  Invertivore  ... #> 3 Erithacus rubecula    11.5        72.3               17.1   partial  Invertivore  ... #> 4    Turdus merula      20.8       130.5               95.0   partial    Omnivore   ... #> 5 Falco peregrinus      15.2       312.0              750.0      full  Vertivore    ... #> 6 Aquila chrysaetos     37.5       607.0             4000.0   partial  Vertivore    ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: EltonTraits 1.0 covers birds mammals (~15,400 species total), making enrichment spans two vertebrate classes. adds 18 columns organized three groups: 10 diet composition percentages, 6 foraging stratum percentages, body mass, nocturnality. diet columns express percentage contribution food category species’ diet: invertebrates (diet_inv), endothermic vertebrates (diet_vend), ectothermic vertebrates (diet_vect), fish (diet_vfish), unknown vertebrates (diet_vunk), scavenging (diet_scav), fruit (diet_fruit), nectar (diet_nect), seeds nuts (diet_seed), plant material (diet_plantother). 10 diet percentages sum 100 species. foraging columns express vertical habitat structure species forages: water surface (foraging_water), ground (foraging_ground), understory (foraging_understory), mid high vegetation (foraging_midhigh), canopy (foraging_canopy), aerial (foraging_aerial). 6 percentages also sum 100. Common Swift (Apus apus) textbook example species extreme foraging diet axes: 100% invertebrate diet, 100% aerial foraging, reflecting life spent almost entirely wing. Barn Owl (Tyto alba) illustrates nocturnal flag: one species example set nocturnal = 1. elton_body_mass_g column provides body mass grams literature compilation. EltonTraits particularly valuable functional diversity analyses (computing Rao’s quadratic entropy functional richness using diet foraging traits axes), food web construction (using diet percentages parameterize trophic links), macroecological studies niche breadth. licensed CC0 published Figshare. reference Wilman et al. (2014), Ecology 95:2027.
code: birds <- taxify(c(   "Parus major", "Dendrocopos major", "Alcedo atthis",   "Tyto alba", "Apus apus" ))  birds |> add_elton_traits() #>       input_name diet_inv diet_fruit diet_seed foraging_canopy foraging_aerial nocturnal ... #> 1    Parus major       60         10        20              50               0         0 ... #> 2 Dendrocopos major    75          5        10              80               0         0 ... #> 3   Alcedo atthis       0          0         0               0               0         0 ... #> 4       Tyto alba       10          0         0               0               0         1 ... #> 5       Apus apus      100          0         0               0             100         0 ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: PanTHERIA covers ~5,400 mammal species eight life-history ecological traits: adult body mass, maximum longevity, mean litter size, gestation length, weaning age, home range size, diet breadth, habitat breadth. remains -cited source mammalian life-history data ecological literature, despite published 2009. body mass column named pantheria_body_mass_g distinguish AVONET’s avonet_body_mass_g EltonTraits’ elton_body_mass_g. prefixing convention prevents column-name collisions stacking multiple enrichments result. Least Weasel (Mustela nivalis) example illustrates dynamic range mammalian traits: 67 g body mass home range 0.03 km^2, sits opposite end spectrum Brown Bear (Ursus arctos) 139,000 g 488 km^2. allometric scaling relationships (body mass predicting home range, longevity, gestation, etc.) major reason PanTHERIA widely cited. PanTHERIA published 2009, species described taxonomically split date appear NA. dataset static (CC0 license), never triggers version checks. reference Jones et al. (2009), Ecology 90:2648.
code: mammals <- taxify(c(   "Vulpes vulpes", "Canis lupus", "Ursus arctos",   "Mustela nivalis", "Lutra lutra", "Lynx lynx" ))  mammals |> add_pantheria() #>      input_name pantheria_body_mass_g longevity_mo litter_size home_range_km2 ... #> 1  Vulpes vulpes               5480.0          144         5.0           8.55 ... #> 2   Canis lupus              31757.0          192         5.4         242.00 ... #> 3  Ursus arctos             139000.0          396         2.0         488.00 ... #> 4 Mustela nivalis               67.0           72         5.5           0.03 ... #> 5   Lutra lutra               8000.0          180         2.3          15.00 ... #> 6     Lynx lynx              20500.0          252         2.6         168.00 ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: AmphiBIO covers ~6,800 amphibian species 13 trait columns. continuous traits body size (snout-vent length mm), age maturity (days), longevity (days), clutch/litter size, reproductive output per year, offspring size (mm). binary traits encode habitat activity patterns: direct development (0/1), larval stage (0/1), aquatic habitat (0/1), fossorial habitat (0/1), arboreal habitat (0/1), diurnal activity (0/1), nocturnal activity (0/1). nocturnality column named nocturnal_amphibio rather nocturnal avoid colliding EltonTraits’ nocturnal column. unusual stack AmphiBIO EltonTraits result (EltonTraits covers birds mammals, amphibians), precaution prevents surprises mixed-taxon workflows enrichments applied single data.frame. binary trait columns use integer values (0/1) rather logical (TRUE/FALSE), following original dataset’s encoding. means filtering syntax uses == 1L rather bare column names: result[result$arboreal == 1L, ]. AmphiBIO one large-scale trait databases amphibians, taxon group relatively data-poor compared birds mammals. Coverage spans anurans (frogs toads), urodeles (salamanders newts), caecilians. licensed CC 4.0 published Scientific Data. reference Oliveira et al. (2017), Scientific Data 4:170123.
code: amphibians <- taxify(c(   "Bufo bufo", "Rana temporaria", "Salamandra salamandra",   "Triturus cristatus", "Hyla arborea", "Bombina variegata" ))  amphibians |> add_amphibio() #>           input_name body_size_mm arboreal aquatic direct_development nocturnal_amphibio ... #> 1          Bufo bufo        150.0        0       0                  0                  1 ... #> 2    Rana temporaria        110.0        0       1                  0                  0 ... #> 3 Salamandra salamandra    200.0        0       0                  0                  1 ... #> 4  Triturus cristatus      160.0        0       1                  0                  1 ... #> 5       Hyla arborea         50.0        1       0                  0                  1 ... #> 6  Bombina variegata         50.0        0       1                  0                  0 ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Fungi historically underrepresented trait databases compared plants animals, two complementary datasets now provide detailed ecological functional information. FungalTraits classifies genera lifestyle, growth form, interaction capabilities, FUNGuild provides guild-based trophic classification. Together offer reasonably complete functional profile macrofungi many microfungi.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: FungalTraits genus-level database covering ~10,200 fungal genera nine trait columns. Unlike species-level enrichments discussed , FungalTraits joins genus rather accepted_name. reflects reality fungal trait data: functional traits (lifestyle, growth form, decay strategy) conserved genus level, enormous diversity described fungal species (~150,000) makes species-level trait compilation impractical many attributes. genus-level join means species within genus receive trait values, ecologically reasonable traits covered. nine trait columns capture complementary facets fungal ecology: primary_lifestyle: dominant trophic strategy (ectomycorrhizal, saprotroph, plant pathogen, animal parasite, lichen, endophyte, etc.). secondary_lifestyle: additional lifestyle applicable (many genera single lifestyle, frequently NA). growth_form: vegetative morphology (agaricoid, boletoid, polyporoid, corticioid, clavarioid, gasteroid, etc.). fruitbody_type: reproductive structure type. decay_substrate: primary substrate saprotrophic genera (wood, litter, dung, soil). plant_pathogenic_capacity: coarse classification pathogenic potential plant-associated genera. animal_biotrophic_capacity: analogous classification animal associations. endophytic_interaction_capability: whether genus includes endophytic species. ectomycorrhiza_exploration_type: ectomycorrhizal genera, exploration type mycelium (contact, short-distance, medium- distance, long-distance). ecologically important exploration type governs nutrient acquisition strategy competitive dynamics among ectomycorrhizal fungi. primary_lifestyle column single informative trait broad ecological analyses. separates ectomycorrhizal fungi (mutualists form nutrient-exchange networks plant roots) saprotrophs (decomposers drive nutrient cycling) pathogens (agents disease mortality). three groups fundamentally different roles ecosystem functioning, knowing lifestyle genus belongs determines interpreted community analyses, food web models, carbon cycling studies. FungalTraits licensed CC 4.0 published Fungal Diversity. reference Polme et al. (2020), Fungal Diversity 105:1-16. dataset classified static taxify manifest.
code: fungi <- taxify(c(   "Amanita muscaria", "Boletus edulis", "Trametes versicolor",   "Agaricus bisporus", "Cantharellus cibarius" ))  fungi |> add_fungal_traits() #>          input_name primary_lifestyle  growth_form fruitbody_type decay_substrate ... #> 1  Amanita muscaria   ectomycorrhizal     agaricoid      agaricoid            <NA> ... #> 2    Boletus edulis   ectomycorrhizal       boletoid       boletoid            <NA> ... #> 3 Trametes versicolor      saprotroph  polyporoid      polyporoid           wood  ... #> 4 Agaricus bisporus       saprotroph     agaricoid      agaricoid          litter ... #> 5 Cantharellus cibarius ectomycorrhizal cantharelloid cantharelloid          <NA> ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: FUNGuild provides trophic guild classifications ~13,000 fungal taxa genus species levels. FungalTraits describes genus ecologically (lifestyle, growth form, substrate preference), FUNGuild classifies taxa guild categories describe functional role ecosystem. two datasets complementary: genus like Trametes might classified “saprotroph” “polyporoid” growth form FungalTraits, “Wood Saprotroph” guild “Saprotroph” trophic mode FUNGuild.
