FUNGuild classification coarser directly interpretable guild-based community analyses. four output columns provide hierarchical classification: trophic_mode: broadest category (Saprotroph, Symbiotroph, Pathotroph, combinations like Saprotroph-Symbiotroph genera multiple trophic strategies). guild: finer classification within trophic mode (Wood Saprotroph, Litter Saprotroph, Ectomycorrhizal, Arbuscular Mycorrhizal, Plant Pathogen, Animal Pathogen, Lichenized, etc.). funguild_growth_form: morphological category, named funguild_ prefix avoid collision FungalTraits’ growth_form column. confidence_ranking: confident guild assignment (Highly Probable, Probable, Possible). column deserves attention: assignments “Possible” level based limited evidence treated caution quantitative analyses. Filtering “Highly Probable” “Probable” assignments reduces coverage improves reliability. FUNGuild particularly valuable soil mycobiome studies, operational taxonomic units (OTUs) metabarcoding classified ecological guilds. trophic mode guild columns map directly onto functional group categories used fungal community ecology: ratio saprotrophs symbiotrophs, proportion pathotrophs community, common response variables studies land use change, nutrient cycling, plant-soil feedbacks. FUNGuild published Fungal Ecology. reference Nguyen et al. (2016), Fungal Ecology 20:241-248. dataset classified static taxify manifest.
code: fungi <- taxify(c(   "Amanita muscaria", "Boletus edulis", "Trametes versicolor",   "Agaricus bisporus", "Cantharellus cibarius" ))  fungi |> add_funguild() #>          input_name     trophic_mode                   guild funguild_growth_form confidence_ranking #> 1  Amanita muscaria       Symbiotroph       Ectomycorrhizal            Agaricoid            Highly Probable #> 2    Boletus edulis       Symbiotroph       Ectomycorrhizal             Boletoid            Highly Probable #> 3 Trametes versicolor      Saprotroph        Wood Saprotroph           Polyporoid            Highly Probable #> 4 Agaricus bisporus       Saprotroph      Litter Saprotroph            Agaricoid            Highly Probable #> 5 Cantharellus cibarius   Symbiotroph       Ectomycorrhizal        Cantharelloid            Highly Probable

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: AlgaeTraits provides morphological ecological traits ~1,745 European macroalgae species (seaweeds). Macroalgae dominant primary producers coastal rocky ecosystems, yet conspicuously absent major plant trait databases (TRY, LEDA, EIVE) focus exclusively vascular plants. AlgaeTraits fills gap European coastline, covering green algae (Chlorophyta), brown algae (Phaeophyceae), red algae (Rhodophyta) eight trait columns spanning morphology, habitat, environmental tolerances. eight columns prefixed algae_ clearly distinguish terrestrial plant traits: algae_body_size_cm: maximum thallus length centimetres. algae_growth_form: morphological category (filamentous, foliose, corticated, leathery, calcareous, crustose, etc.). algae_calcification: whether species produces calcium carbonate structures (none, articulated, crustose). Calcifying algae like coralline species play critical role reef construction particularly sensitive ocean acidification. algae_life_span: typical life span category (annual, perennial, pseudoperennial). algae_tidal_zone: primary tidal zone (supralittoral, intertidal, subtidal). algae_wave_exposure: preferred wave exposure regime (sheltered, moderately exposed, exposed). algae_environment: salinity regime (marine, brackish, freshwater). algae_substrate: preferred substrate type (rock, sand, epiphytic, free-living). body size column spans three orders magnitude, millimetre-scale filamentous algae kelps exceeding 3 metres. variation underpins structural complexity rocky shore communities: large canopy-forming species like Laminaria digitata create habitat hundreds associated species, small turf-forming species dominate disturbed nutrient-enriched conditions. growth form tidal zone columns together define ecological niche species along shore gradient, making AlgaeTraits directly useful intertidal community analyses, climate change impact assessments, marine protected area planning. geographic scope European, species coastlines receive NA columns. dataset licensed CC 4.0 published Scientific Data. reference Vranken et al. (2023), Scientific Data 10:826. dataset classified static taxify manifest.
code: seaweeds <- taxify(c(   "Fucus vesiculosus", "Ulva lactuca", "Laminaria digitata",   "Chondrus crispus", "Sargassum muticum" ))  seaweeds |> add_algae_traits() #>          input_name algae_body_size_cm algae_growth_form algae_calcification algae_tidal_zone ... #> 1 Fucus vesiculosus               60.0          foliose               none         intertidal ... #> 2      Ulva lactuca               30.0          foliose               none         intertidal ... #> 3 Laminaria digitata             200.0          foliose               none         subtidal   ... #> 4    Chondrus crispus              15.0          foliose               none         intertidal ... #> 5  Sargassum muticum             300.0          foliose               none         subtidal   ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Fish ecology produced two large, complementary trait databases. FISHMORPH focuses morphological measurements freshwater species, FishBase provides broader ecological life-history data freshwater marine fish. Together provide detailed functional profiles ichthyological studies.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: FISHMORPH provides morphological trait data ~8,300 freshwater fish species worldwide, based standardized measurements photographs museum specimens. 10 morphological traits capture key axes fish body shape variation determine swimming performance, feeding mode, habitat use. columns prefixed fish_ express dimensionless morphological ratios normalized body length. 10 traits : fish_body_elongation: body depth relative standard length. High values indicate deep-bodied species (cyprinids), low values indicate elongated species (eels, pike). fish_eye_size: eye diameter relative head length. Large-eyed species tend visual predators clear water. fish_oral_gape_position: vertical position mouth, ventral (benthic feeders) dorsal (surface feeders). fish_body_lateral_shape: lateral compression body. fish_pectoral_fin_size: pectoral fin area, associated manoeuvrability braking ability. fish_pectoral_fin_position: vertical insertion pectoral fin body. fish_caudal_peduncle_throttling: narrowing caudal peduncle, associated sustained swimming efficiency. fish_caudal_fin_shape: aspect ratio caudal fin. High values (forked tails) indicate cruising swimmers; low values (rounded tails) indicate ambush predators benthic species. fish_fin_surface_ratio: total fin area relative body area. fish_max_body_length_cm: maximum recorded standard length centimetres. morphological ratios ecomorphological indicators: predict species interacts physical environment. Body elongation caudal fin shape together separate benthic, slow-moving species pelagic, fast-cruising species. Oral gape position separates surface feeders bottom feeders. Eye size pectoral fin size relate sensory ecology manoeuvrability, respectively. combination traits places species morphological space, making FISHMORPH directly useful functional diversity calculations (Rao’s Q, functional richness, functional divergence) freshwater fish community ecology. dataset covers freshwater fish ; marine species receive NA. licensed CC 4.0 published Global Ecology Biogeography. reference Brosse et al. (2021), Global Ecology Biogeography 30:2330-2345. dataset classified static taxify manifest.
code: freshwater_fish <- taxify(c(   "Salmo trutta", "Esox lucius", "Cyprinus carpio",   "Perca fluviatilis", "Silurus glanis" ))  freshwater_fish |> add_fish_traits() #>        input_name fish_body_elongation fish_eye_size fish_oral_gape_position fish_body_lateral_shape ... #> 1    Salmo trutta                 0.22          0.08                    0.42                    0.18 ... #> 2    Esox lucius                  0.18          0.06                    0.50                    0.15 ... #> 3  Cyprinus carpio                0.35          0.05                    0.38                    0.25 ... #> 4 Perca fluviatilis               0.30          0.07                    0.40                    0.22 ... #> 5   Silurus glanis                0.15          0.03                    0.48                    0.12 ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: FishBase comprehensive fish database world, covering ~35,000 species across freshwater marine environments. taxify enrichment extracts eight key ecological life-history traits FishBase dataset, providing broad functional profile complements FISHMORPH’s morphological focus. eight columns prefixed fb_: fb_body_length_cm: maximum total length centimetres. fb_body_mass_g: maximum recorded body mass grams. fb_trophic_level: trophic level (continuous, typically 2.0-5.0). Herbivorous fish sit near 2.0, planktivores around 3.0, piscivores around 4.0-4.5, apex predators 4.5. fb_depth_min_m fb_depth_max_m: minimum maximum depth range metres. Together define vertical habitat envelope. fb_vulnerability: intrinsic vulnerability index (0-100), composite score based maximum size, age, fecundity, life-history parameters. High values indicate species inherently susceptible overexploitation. fb_habitat: primary habitat category (pelagic, demersal, bathydemersal, bathypelagic, reef-associated, etc.). fb_importance: economic importance category (commercial, subsistence, minor commercial, gamefish, etc.). trophic level vulnerability columns particularly valuable fisheries ecology marine conservation. Trophic level quantifies position species food web, well-documented pattern “fishing food web” (declining mean trophic level catches time) diagnosed using exactly variable. Vulnerability provides quick assessment species community risk fishing pressure, complementing IUCN conservation status mechanistic, trait-based risk metric. Note FishBase licensed CC -NC 3.0 (non-commercial use). restrictive CC 4.0 license used enrichments. Users intending use FishBase data commercial applications consult FishBase terms use. reference Froese, R. D. Pauly (2024), FishBase, www.fishbase.org. dataset classified non-static taxify manifest FishBase updated periodically.
code: fish <- taxify(c(   "Gadus morhua", "Thunnus thynnus", "Hippocampus hippocampus",   "Squalus acanthias", "Salmo trutta" ))  fish |> add_fishbase() #>              input_name fb_body_length_cm fb_body_mass_g fb_trophic_level fb_depth_min_m fb_depth_max_m ... #> 1          Gadus morhua            132.0        55500.0              4.4            0.0          600.0 ... #> 2      Thunnus thynnus            458.0       684000.0              4.2            0.0         1000.0 ... #> 3 Hippocampus hippocampus          15.0             NA              3.1            1.0           60.0 ... #> 4    Squalus acanthias            160.0         11000.0              4.3           16.0          900.0 ... #> 5         Salmo trutta            140.0        50000.0              3.4            0.0          332.0 ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: ReptTraits covers 12,060 reptile species across reptile groups: snakes, lizards, amphisbaenians, turtles, crocodiles, tuatara. built Reptile Database taxonomy, joins cleanly reptiledb backbone. Alongside body-size life-history traits carries per-species distribution signal: biogeographic realm, elevation range, mean climate. distribution environment columns per-species range signal: biogeographic_realm: main biogeographic realm (Neotropic, Oriental, Australo-Pacific, Afrotropic, Palearctic, Nearctic, Saharo-Sindian, Madagascan, Marine). microhabitat: microhabitat use (terrestrial, saxicolous, arboreal, fossorial, aquatic, combinations). habitat_type: broad habitat type(s) (forest, desert, grassland, shrubland, savanna, wetlands). elevation_min_m, elevation_max_m: recorded elevation range metres. mean_annual_temp_c: mean annual temperature across range, degrees Celsius. insular_endemic: whether species insular/endemic. realm-level range coarse. useful filter “species broadly occur”, fine-grained (TDWG-level) range like plant ranges drive region constraint taxify(). morphology life-history columns follow: body_mass_g: maximum body mass grams. svl_mm: maximum snout-vent length (straight carapace length turtles) millimetres. total_length_mm: maximum total length millimetres. longevity_yr: maximum recorded longevity years. diet: diet category (carnivorous, herbivorous, omnivorous). reproductive_mode: oviparous, viviparous, ovoviviparous. clutch_size: mean clutch litter size. active_time: activity period (diurnal, nocturnal, cathemeral). foraging_mode: foraging strategy (active, ambush, mixed). Species absent ReptTraits receive NA columns. licensed CC 4.0 published Scientific Data. reference Oskyrko, Mi, Meiri & Du (2024), Scientific Data 11:243. dataset classified static taxify manifest.
code: reptiles <- taxify(c(   "Pogona vitticeps", "Python regius", "Chelonia mydas",   "Naja naja", "Crocodylus niloticus" ), backend = "reptiledb")  reptiles |> add_repttraits() #>             input_name biogeographic_realm habitat_type elevation_max_m body_mass_g  svl_mm reproductive_mode ... #> 1     Pogona vitticeps     Australo-Pacific      Desert/...             612       575.4   250         oviparous ... #> 2        Python regius          Afrotropic         Forest            1200      1610.6  1125         oviparous ... #> 3       Chelonia mydas              Marine       Wetlands              NA    230000.0  1500         oviparous ... #> 4            Naja naja            Oriental         Forest            1009      1683.5  1570         oviparous ... #> 5 Crocodylus niloticus          Afrotropic       Wetlands              NA    750000.0    NA         oviparous ...

what: AnAge longevity and life-history (Tacutu et al. 2018)
title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: AnAge curated database aging longevity records ~4,700 vertebrate species spanning mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, fish. provides maximum longevity, body mass, metabolic rate, maturity age, gestation/incubation time, litter/clutch size, birth mass, growth rate, body temperature. unique value AnAge taxon-specific databases like PanTHERIA cross-class coverage: longevity metabolic data can compared directly across vertebrate classes. columns use anage_ prefix body mass litter size distinguish PanTHERIA equivalents. max_longevity_yr column maximum recorded lifespan years — widely used parameter cross-species aging comparisons. dataset compiled Human Ageing Genomic Resources (HAGR) freely available CC . reference Tacutu et al. (2018), Nucleic Acids Research 46:D1083-D1090.
code: vertebrates <- taxify(c(   "Vulpes vulpes", "Aquila chrysaetos", "Crocodylus niloticus",   "Bufo bufo", "Salmo salar" ), backend = c("col", "gbif"))  vertebrates |> add_anage() #>              input_name max_longevity_yr anage_body_mass_g metabolic_rate_w ... #> 1         Vulpes vulpes             15.2            5480.0            10.41 ... #> 2   Aquila chrysaetos              46.0            4210.0             8.94 ... #> 3 Crocodylus niloticus             44.0          242500.0               NA ... #> 4            Bufo bufo             36.0              48.0               NA ... #> 5         Salmo salar              13.0            3400.0               NA ...

what: AnimalTraits body mass and metabolic rate (Hebert et al. 2022)
title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: AnimalTraits curated database body mass metabolic rate measurements covering ~2,000 species across arthropods (~1,700 species), vertebrates, molluscs, annelids. Unlike taxon-specific databases, provides unified framework cross-taxon allometric comparisons — particularly valuable arthropods, underrepresented trait databases. data stored individual-level observations source CSV; taxify’s parse function aggregates species-level medians. Body mass kilograms metabolic rate watts (SI units, published). animaltraits_ prefix avoids collision body mass columns enrichments. dataset licensed CC0 (public domain) published Zenodo. reference Hebert et al. (2022), Scientific Data 9:265.
code: arthropods <- taxify(c(   "Drosophila melanogaster", "Apis mellifera",   "Tenebrio molitor", "Gryllus campestris" ), backend = c("col", "gbif"))  arthropods |> add_animaltraits() #>                input_name animaltraits_body_mass_kg animaltraits_metabolic_rate_w #> 1 Drosophila melanogaster              0.000001030                    0.000000218 #> 2         Apis mellifera              0.000100000                    0.000012600 #> 3       Tenebrio molitor              0.000140000                    0.000004850 #> 4    Gryllus campestris              0.000800000                           NA

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: LepTraits 1.0 comprehensive open butterfly trait database, covering ~12,400 species Papilionoidea globally. provides wingspan, voltinism, diapause stage, four habitat affinity dimensions, host plant data, adult flight phenology. wingspan computed midpoint lower upper bounds reported dataset. Voltinism indicates number generations per year. four habitat affinities (canopy, edge, moisture, disturbance) categorical variables describing species’ preferred environmental context. flight_months column counts number months recorded adult flight activity. dataset licensed CC0 published Figshare. reference Shirey et al. (2022), Scientific Data 9:398.
code: butterflies <- taxify(c(   "Vanessa cardui", "Pieris rapae", "Papilio machaon",   "Lycaena phlaeas", "Colias crocea" ), backend = c("col", "gbif"))  butterflies |> add_leptraits() #>        input_name wingspan_mm voltinism diapause_stage canopy_affinity ... #> 1   Vanessa cardui        62.5       3.0             NA    Open canopy ... #> 2     Pieris rapae        47.5       4.0           pupa    Open canopy ... #> 3  Papilio machaon        75.0       2.0           pupa    Open canopy ... #> 4  Lycaena phlaeas        30.0       3.0          larva    Open canopy ... #> 5    Colias crocea        48.5       3.0          adult    Open canopy ...

what: NW European Arthropod life-history traits (Logghe et al. 2025)
title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: dataset provides 28 life-history ecological traits ~4,900 arthropod species Northwestern Europe, covering 10 orders including Coleoptera, Hemiptera, Orthoptera, Araneae, Diptera, Hymenoptera, Lepidoptera. comprehensive open arthropod trait compilation region. columns prefixed arthropod_. quantitative traits include body size (mm), dispersal ability (0-1 ratio within order), mean voltinism, fecundity, development time (days), lifespan (days), thermal niche mean (°C). categorical traits include diurnality, feeding guild, trophic range. dataset geographically scoped NW Europe (Belgium, Luxembourg, Netherlands, northern France, UK, western Germany), species regions NA values. dataset particularly strong Coleoptera, Hemiptera, Orthoptera, near-complete coverage regional fauna orders. dataset licensed CC -NC published Biodiversity Data Journal. reference Logghe et al. (2025), Biodiversity Data Journal 13:e146785.
code: insects <- taxify(c(   "Abax parallelepipedus", "Pterostichus melanarius",   "Chorthippus parallelus", "Araneus diadematus" ), backend = c("col", "gbif"))  insects |> add_arthropod_traits() #>               input_name arthropod_body_size_mm arthropod_dispersal arthropod_voltinism arthropod_feeding_guild ... #> 1 Abax parallelepipedus                   18.5                0.01                 1.0               carnivore ... #> 2 Pterostichus melanarius                 15.0                0.32                 1.0               carnivore ... #> 3 Chorthippus parallelus                  17.0                0.10                 1.0               herbivore ... #> 4   Araneus diadematus                    13.0                0.45                 1.0               carnivore ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Five enrichments filter grouping variable (country code, TDWG botanical region code, GloNAF region code, language code) pivot result wide format. mechanics across : single group value requested, output column uses base name (e.g., invasive_status). multiple group values requested, output column gets suffix derived group value (e.g., invasive_status_AT, invasive_status_DE). Passing "" group value expands every group present enrichment dataset. design keeps output tidy common case (one country, one region, one language) still supporting comparative analyses across multiple groups without reshaping data manually. Internally, group-based join performs one match() call per group value, requesting 10 countries costs roughly 10 times computation single country. still fast typical use cases (sub-second results tens thousands rows), requesting "" large result may take seconds iterates group values enrichment (196 countries GRIIS, dozens TDWG regions WCVP).

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Global Register Introduced Invasive Species (GRIIS) classifies species native, introduced, invasive per-country basis. dataset covers 196 countries ~23,000 species-country combinations. country argument takes ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 codes (e.g., "" Austria, "DE" Germany, "GB" Great Britain).

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: single country code, output column simply invasive_status without suffix. three possible values "native", "introduced", "invasive". Species recorded GRIIS dataset requested country receive NA. Note NA mean “native”; means “record” GRIIS database. Many native species simply listed GRIIS focuses introduced invasive taxa.
code: plants <- taxify(c(   "Robinia pseudoacacia", "Ailanthus altissima",   "Impatiens glandulifera", "Quercus robur",   "Reynoutria japonica", "Solidago canadensis" ))  plants |> add_invasive_status(country = "AT") #>            input_name invasive_status #> 1 Robinia pseudoacacia        invasive #> 2  Ailanthus altissima        invasive #> 3 Impatiens glandulifera      invasive #> 4        Quercus robur          native #> 5  Reynoutria japonica        invasive #> 6 Solidago canadensis        invasive

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: comparing invasive status across countries, pass vector codes. output column suffixed corresponding country code. layout makes cross-country comparisons straightforward. Filtering species differ status countries matter subsetting columns. example finds species classified invasive Austria (yet) classified invasive Germany: pattern useful identifying species may expanding invasive range, comparing regulatory status non-native species across neighboring countries.
code: plants |> add_invasive_status(country = c("AT", "DE", "GB")) #>            input_name invasive_status_AT invasive_status_DE invasive_status_GB #> 1 Robinia pseudoacacia         invasive           invasive           invasive #> 2  Ailanthus altissima         invasive           invasive         introduced #> 3 Impatiens glandulifera       invasive           invasive           invasive #> 4        Quercus robur           native             native             native #> 5  Reynoutria japonica         invasive           invasive           invasive #> 6 Solidago canadensis         invasive           invasive         introduced result <- plants |> add_invasive_status(country = c("AT", "DE")) # Species invasive in Austria but not in Germany result[result$invasive_status_AT == "invasive" &        result$invasive_status_DE != "invasive", ]

title: Enriching results with trait and status data

what: Alien species first records (Seebens et al.)
title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Global Alien Species First Record Database (Seebens et al. 2017) records year alien species first documented given country territory. Unlike GRIIS (records current status), enrichment provides historical timeline alien species arrivals. dataset covers taxa (plants, animals, fungi) ~77,000 species-country combinations across 241 countries. country argument takes ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 codes, add_invasive_status().

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: row gets three columns: alien_first_record (year integer), alien_first_record_source (database contributed record, e.g., “NOBANIS”, “GAVIA”, “FishBase”), alien_first_record_reference (original citation). Native species like Quercus robur receive NA alien first records database. source reference columns provide row-level provenance. matters second first-records source (GBIF occurrence-based records) added future version, source column distinguish database contributed record.
code: aliens <- taxify(c(   "Robinia pseudoacacia", "Ailanthus altissima",   "Impatiens glandulifera", "Quercus robur",   "Ambrosia artemisiifolia", "Solidago canadensis" ))  aliens |> add_alien_first_records(country = "AT") #>              input_name alien_first_record alien_first_record_source alien_first_record_reference #> 1   Robinia pseudoacacia               1850                   NOBANIS                      NOBANIS #> 2    Ailanthus altissima               1870                   NOBANIS                      NOBANIS #> 3 Impatiens glandulifera               1900                   NOBANIS                      NOBANIS #> 4          Quercus robur                 NA                      <NA>                         <NA> #> 5 Ambrosia artemisiifolia              1863                   NOBANIS                      NOBANIS #> 6  Solidago canadensis                 1850                   NOBANIS                      NOBANIS

title: Enriching results with trait and status data

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: World Checklist Vascular Plants (WCVP) Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew, classifies ~340,000 plant species native, introduced, extinct TDWG Level 2 botanical regions. TDWG (Taxonomic Databases Working Group, now TDWG Biodiversity Information Standards) defined hierarchical system geographic regions recording plant distributions. Level 2 regions continent-scale units. region argument takes TDWG Level 2 codes. Common codes include: EUR (Europe) NAM (Northern America) SAM (Southern America) AFR (Africa) AUS (Australasia) ASI (Asia-Temperate) AST (Asia-Tropical) PAC (Pacific) ANT (Antarctica) Eucalyptus globulus returns NA Europe native Australia, absent WCVP dataset. dataset records species natively distributed, planted naturalized. important distinction: many cultivated species show NA regions widespread gardens plantations. Querying multiple regions reveals species’ native continental range: Sequoiadendron giganteum (Giant Sequoia) returns NA three regions native western North America (NAM), included query. illustrates absence region code query mean species lacks native range data; means ask right region. full list available TDWG codes can retrieved programmatically manifest (available_groups field wcvp enrichment), TDWG geographic standard documentation. WCVP non-static: Kew updates checklist periodically, taxify enrichment rebuilt new versions published.
code: trees <- taxify(c(   "Quercus robur", "Quercus suber", "Eucalyptus globulus",   "Nothofagus pumilio", "Sequoiadendron giganteum" ))  trees |> add_wcvp(region = "EUR") #>              input_name native_status #> 1          Quercus robur        native #> 2          Quercus suber        native #> 3    Eucalyptus globulus            NA #> 4    Nothofagus pumilio            NA #> 5 Sequoiadendron giganteum          NA trees |> add_wcvp(region = c("EUR", "AUS", "SAM")) #>              input_name native_status_EUR native_status_AUS native_status_SAM #> 1          Quercus robur           native                NA                NA #> 2          Quercus suber           native                NA                NA #> 3    Eucalyptus globulus               NA            native                NA #> 4    Nothofagus pumilio               NA                NA            native #> 5 Sequoiadendron giganteum              NA                NA                NA

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Global Naturalized Alien Flora (GloNAF) records plant species naturalized ~1,300 regions worldwide. Unlike GRIIS (classifies species native/introduced/invasive per country), GloNAF provides binary naturalization flag per region finer geographic resolution, using TDWG-compatible codes extended dot notation sub-national units (e.g., "USA.CA" California). output column naturalized 1 species recorded naturalized queried region, NA otherwise. Multiple regions produce suffixed columns (naturalized_EUR, naturalized_NAM). region = "" option expands ~1,300 regions. GloNAF complements GRIIS: GRIIS provides invasion status dimension (native/introduced/invasive), GloNAF provides geographic coverage dimension (species established self-sustaining populations?). Combining gives fuller picture alien plant distributions. dataset licensed CC 4.0. reference van Kleunen et al. (2019), Ecology 100:e02542 (v1.0) Davis et al. (2025), Ecology e70245 (v2.0). GloNAF classified static taxify manifest.
code: plants <- taxify(c(   "Robinia pseudoacacia", "Ailanthus altissima",   "Impatiens glandulifera", "Quercus robur" ))  plants |> add_glonaf(region = "EUR") #>              input_name naturalized #> 1   Robinia pseudoacacia           1 #> 2    Ailanthus altissima           1 #> 3 Impatiens glandulifera           1 #> 4          Quercus robur          NA

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: common names enrichment draws GBIF’s vernacular name database, aggregates names many national regional nomenclature sources. multilingual enrichments, covering dozens languages. lang argument takes ISO 639-1 two-letter language codes. default language English (lang = "en"). Switching another language matter changing lang argument: multiple common names exist species requested language, first (commonly used) entry returned. Coverage varies substantially language: English German broadest coverage (European widespread species entries). French, Spanish, Portuguese, Dutch also good coverage. Less widely spoken languages languages limited digital biodiversity infrastructure may gaps, resulting NA species common names languages digitized GBIF’s aggregation. common names enrichment non-static (GBIF updates backbone periodically) licensed CC0. single language requested, output column common_name. multiple languages supported single call, follow group-based suffix pattern, practice common usage pattern one language per call.
code: species <- taxify(c(   "Quercus robur", "Parus major", "Vulpes vulpes",   "Bufo bufo", "Picea abies" ))  species |> add_common_names() #>     input_name common_name #> 1 Quercus robur   Pedunculate Oak #> 2   Parus major    Great Tit #> 3  Vulpes vulpes    Red Fox #> 4     Bufo bufo    Common Toad #> 5   Picea abies    Norway Spruce species |> add_common_names(lang = "de") #>     input_name common_name #> 1 Quercus robur   Stieleiche #> 2   Parus major    Kohlmeise #> 3  Vulpes vulpes    Rotfuchs #> 4     Bufo bufo    Erdkroete #> 5   Picea abies    Gemeine Fichte

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: add_*() functions return data.frame class (taxify_result) receive, preserving attributes including metadata used summary(). means compose naturally pipe operator. typical workflow chains taxify() several enrichment calls, building desired set columns incrementally. enrichment appends columns right data.frame. result chain original 16 taxify columns plus conservation_status, woodiness, five EIVE columns (eive_light, eive_temperature, eive_moisture, eive_reaction, eive_nutrients), two Diaz columns (seed_mass_mg, plant_height_m). 25 columns total. Order within chain affect output enrichment operates independently accepted_name column. case order matters column-name collision LEDA Diaz (seed_mass_mg), discussed earlier. similar chain birds, combining morphological measurements diet data vernacular names: produces 16 (base) + 1 (conservation) + 11 (AVONET) + 18 (EltonTraits) + 1 (common name) = 47 columns. AVONET EltonTraits contribute body mass, distinct columns (avonet_body_mass_g elton_body_mass_g), overwriting. mammals, combining life-history traits PanTHERIA diet data EltonTraits German common names: EltonTraits PanTHERIA cover mammals, contribute data mammal chain. EltonTraits provides diet composition percentages foraging strata; PanTHERIA provides life-history traits like longevity, litter size, home range. combination gives multidimensional view species’ ecology without manual data assembly. pattern works fungi, combining lifestyle traits FungalTraits guild classifications FUNGuild: FungalTraits provides detailed ecological traits (lifestyle, growth form, substrate, mycorrhizal exploration type), FUNGuild adds trophic mode guild classification. two enrichments complementary column sets, overwriting except growth form, distinguished funguild_growth_form prefix FUNGuild. confidence_ranking column FUNGuild useful quality filter: restricting “Highly Probable” assignments downstream analysis reduces noise. Fish analyses can similarly combine morphological ecological enrichments: FISHMORPH provides morphological ratios (body elongation, fin shape, eye size) define ecomorphological profile species, FishBase adds ecological life-history context (trophic level, depth range, vulnerability). Note Gadus morhua (Atlantic Cod) NA values FISHMORPH columns FISHMORPH covers freshwater species , fully populated FishBase. kind partial coverage across complementary enrichments expected easy diagnose summary() output. Enrichment chains can long needed. Performance linear number enrichments: add_*() call performs one join, regardless many enrichments already applied. chain 10 enrichments 50,000-row result completes seconds. enrichment files loaded via vectra’s memory-mapped /O, even enrichments hundreds thousands rows (like WCVP ~340,000) consume large amounts RAM. pipe chain pattern also plays well reproducibility workflows. entire analysis, raw species list fully enriched table, captured single, readable expression. Saving code alongside session info (including taxify version enrichment versions summary()) gives complete record data used produce results.
code: library(taxify)  plant_result <- taxify(c(   "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Picea abies",   "Arrhenatherum elatius", "Festuca rubra", "Plantago lanceolata" )) |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_woodiness() |>   add_eive() |>   add_diaz_traits() bird_result <- taxify(c(   "Parus major", "Cyanistes caeruleus", "Erithacus rubecula",   "Turdus merula", "Falco peregrinus" )) |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_avonet() |>   add_elton_traits() |>   add_common_names() mammal_result <- taxify(c(   "Vulpes vulpes", "Canis lupus", "Ursus arctos",   "Lutra lutra", "Lynx lynx" )) |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_pantheria() |>   add_elton_traits() |>   add_common_names(lang = "de") fungal_result <- taxify(c(   "Amanita muscaria", "Boletus edulis", "Trametes versicolor" )) |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_fungal_traits() |>   add_funguild() fish_result <- taxify(c(   "Salmo trutta", "Esox lucius", "Gadus morhua" )) |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_fish_traits() |>   add_fishbase()

what: The enrichment register in summary() output
title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Every add_*() call records metadata enrichment attribute (taxify_meta) result data.frame. metadata includes enrichment name, source label, version string, count rows received non-NA trait values. Calling summary() taxify result displays information alongside standard match statistics, providing compact overview entire analysis pipeline. enrichment register lists applied enrichment source name, version, fraction successfully matched names. example, three enrichments achieved 100% coverage (3 3 matched), expected well-known European tree species. larger dataset broader taxonomic scope, typically see lower fractions, especially enrichments narrow geographic taxonomic coverage. register cumulative: applying enrichments adds lines. makes summary() useful diagnostic end pipe chain. one enrichment shows unexpectedly low coverage (e.g., “2 500 matched” EIVE dataset expected European plants), signals problem worth investigating. Common causes include species list containing non-plant taxa, non-European species, names ranks species. version strings register provide exact provenance information methods section paper. Rather writing “used IUCN Red List” (version? downloaded ?), can report version string directly summary() (e.g., “IUCN Red List v2025.1 distributed taxify enrichment conservation_status v2025.04”).
code: result <- taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Pinus sylvestris")) |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_woodiness() |>   add_eive()  summary(result) #> -- taxify results -------------------------------------------------------- #>   backend: WFO v2024.12  |  3 names submitted #> #>   matched       3  (exact: 3, case-insensitive: 0, fuzzy: 0) #>   -------------------------------------------------------- #>   taxon groups: plant: 3 #> #>   enrichments: #>     conservation_status  (IUCN Red List 2025.1)     -- 3 of 3 matched #>     woodiness            (Zanne et al. 2014 1.0)    -- 3 of 3 matched #>     eive                 (EIVE 1.0 2023.1)          -- 3 of 3 matched

text: dataset spans multiple kingdoms (e.g., biodiversity survey plants, birds, mammals), two strategies. first apply relevant enrichments full result accept NA values taxonomic scope overlap: produces wide data.frame cells given row NA (plant row woodiness data NA beak length, body mass, etc.). advantage simplicity: one data.frame, one summary() call, manual splitting. second strategy split result taxon group, enrich subset appropriate stack, recombine: second approach avoids wide data.frames many NA columns produces cleaner trait matrices downstream analyses treat columns features (ordination, clustering, machine learning). disadvantage code need maintain separate data.frames group. choice depends analysis goal. summary table paper (e.g., “species, conservation status, key traits”), first approach works well. functional diversity calculations trait-based models expect complete trait matrix, second approach typically produces better inputs avoids rows structurally missing values (values missing design, data limitation). middle ground apply add_conservation_status() add_common_names() full dataset (since cover taxon groups), split group taxon-specific enrichments. gives us conservation data vernacular names every species single data.frame, keeping taxon-specific trait matrices clean.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Beyond built-enrichments, add_data() joins external dataset taxify result. accepts file path (CSV, CSV.GZ, XLSX, SQLite/DB, VTR) -memory data.frame. function identifies species name column (automatically running first 10 rows character column taxify() selecting column highest match rate, via explicit species_col argument), resolves names backbone(s) used original taxify() call, joins accepted_id. add_data() resolves names backbone joining, handles synonyms correctly. external data uses “Pinus abies” backbone resolves “Picea abies”, join still works. recommended way integrate local field data, unpublished trait measurements, datasets sources covered built-enrichments. cols argument can restrict columns joined external data. external data 50 columns need two, passing cols = c("bark_thickness_mm", "shade_tolerance") avoids cluttering result unwanted columns. fuzzy argument (default TRUE) enables fuzzy matching names external data exact-match backbone; fuzzy_threshold controls maximum allowed string distance. Column names external data collide existing columns taxify result automatically prefixed "data_" prevent overwriting. multiple rows external data resolve accepted_id identical trait values, deduplicated. resolve accepted_id conflicting values (e.g., two different height measurements species), add_data() raises error asking user resolve ambiguity joining. strict handling duplicates prevents row duplication plain merge() produce. add_data() function also supports SQLite databases via table argument, .vtr files directly (useful sharing pre-built enrichments collaborators). XLSX files, openxlsx2 package required (listed Suggests).
code: result <- taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris", "Fagus sylvatica"))  # From a CSV file (auto-detect species column) result |> add_data("my_traits.csv") # From a data.frame with explicit species column my_traits <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris", "Fagus sylvatica"),   bark_thickness_mm = c(25, 15, 8),   shade_tolerance = c(0.6, 0.3, 0.8) ) result |> add_data(my_traits, species_col = "species")

Citing correct source version professional obligation using data publications. summary() output includes source version applied enrichment, providing starting point methods section. original references listed add_*() function’s help page (accessible via ?add_avonet, ?add_leda, ?add_pantheria, etc.). reproducibility, version recorded meta.json pins exact build enrichment .vtr file used. Static enrichments (Zanne 2014, PanTHERIA 2009, EltonTraits 2014, AmphiBIO 2017, LEDA 2008, Diaz 2022, Seebens 2017, FungalTraits 2020, FUNGuild 2016, AlgaeTraits 2023, FISHMORPH 2021, Meiri 2018, LepTraits 2022, AnimalTraits 2022, NW European Arthropods 2025, GloNAF 2019) fixed versions never change. title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: taxify’s enrichment system turns taxonomic name matching gateway ecological trait data. 22 built-enrichments cover conservation status, growth form, ecological niches, functional traits, diet, morphology, life-history, geographic ranges, invasive status, vernacular names across plants, birds, mammals, amphibians, vertebrates, butterflies, arthropods, fungi, algae, fish, reptiles. enrichments share underlying join mechanics, download automatically first use, cache locally subsequent sessions, compose freely pipe operator. cross-backbone name resolution built .vtr files means worry backbone used: enrichments work identically WFO, COL, GBIF, ITIS, NCBI, OTT, WoRMS results. summary() method tracks enrichments applied, source versions, coverage rates, supporting exploratory analysis reproducible reporting. taxa traits covered built-layers, add_data() integrates external dataset using backbone-resolved name matching. built-enrichments add_data() escape hatch, common ecological analyses can go raw species lists trait-enriched analytical tables single pipe chain. key properties enrichment system, recap, : automatic download caching (manual data management), cross-backbone compatibility (enrichments work regardless backend produced result), version tracking (summary() method documents exactly data versions used), compositional design (enrichments stack freely via pipe operator without side effects ordering constraints). properties together aim make path species names trait-enriched analyses short reproducible possible.

what: What fuzzy matching does (and does not do)
title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: taxify() receives name find exact match, falls back fuzzy matching. Fuzzy matching computes string distance input name every candidate backbone, returns closest candidate whose distance falls threshold. backbone genus-blocked step: names sharing genus compared, keeps search fast even backbones millions rows. String distance purely mechanical measure. counts character-level edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions, optionally transpositions) required transform one string another. fuzzy match tells us two strings spelled similarly. tell us anything whether two names refer biological entity. Fuzzy matching catches typos, transliteration errors, OCR artefacts. resolve taxonomic disagreements, bridge gap common names Latin binomials. distinction matters interpretation. fuzzy match low distance (say 0.05) almost certainly corrects minor typo. fuzzy match high distance (say 0.18) might correct larger OCR error, might matched wrong species entirely. fuzzy_dist column output can tell apart. matching pipeline taxify runs strict sequence: name cleaning first, exact matching (case-sensitive, case-insensitive, Latin orthographic normalization, infraspecific--species fallback), fuzzy matching names survived exact passes without hit. Fuzzy matching never overrides exact match. cleaned input matches backbone name exactly, result stands regardless whether closer fuzzy candidate might exist different spelling. means fuzzy matching step operates genuinely misspelled garbled names.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: taxify supports three string distance algorithms, selected via fuzzy_method argument. three computed C level inside vectra’s fuzzy_join(), runs genus-blocked comparisons parallel via OpenMP.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Damerau-Levenshtein counts four edit operations, costing 1: Insertion: add character (Querus Quercus) Deletion: remove character (Quercuss Quercus) Substitution: replace one character another (Quarcus Quercus) Transposition: swap two adjacent characters (Qurecus Quercus) transposition operation distinguishes Damerau-Levenshtein plain Levenshtein. Transpositions among common typos hand-entered data, treating single edit (rather two: deletion plus insertion) produces tighter distances real-world errors. default good reason: handles common failure modes smallest distance penalty.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Levenshtein supports three operations: insertion, deletion, substitution. transposition like Qurecus Quercus costs 2 edits (delete r, insert r right position) instead 1 edit Damerau-Levenshtein assign. practice means Levenshtein stricter Damerau-Levenshtein transposition-heavy errors identical everything else. threshold value reject candidates Damerau-Levenshtein accept. Levenshtein reasonable choice input data comes controlled source (database export, curated checklist) transpositions rare. OCR hand-typed data, Damerau-Levenshtein almost always better.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Jaro-Winkler fundamentally different edit-distance methods. computes similarity score 0 (completely different) 1 (identical), taxify converts distance 1 - similarity. algorithm gives extra weight characters match beginning string, reflects useful observation taxonomic names: genus informative part, prefix errors rarer epithet errors. Jaro-Winkler operates 0--1 scale definition, fractional thresholds supported. Passing integer threshold (like fuzzy_threshold = 2) fuzzy_method = "jw" raises error immediately. Jaro-Winkler can useful short names (3-5 characters) single edit produces large normalized distance Damerau-Levenshtein, datasets errors concentrated epithet rather genus. general-purpose matching, Damerau-Levenshtein remains safer default.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: fuzzy_threshold argument controls different two strings can match rejected. operates two modes, depending value.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: default threshold 0.2 means: normalized distance must exceed 0.2. Normalized distance defined : scales name length. 5-character name (Abies) gets 1 edit threshold 0.2, 1 / 5 = 0.2. 12-character name (Taraxacum -) gets 2 edits, 2 / 12 = 0.167 < 0.2 3 / 12 = 0.25 > 0.2. 20-character name gets 4 edits. concrete arithmetic representative names: “max edits” column floor(length * threshold). practice comparison uses floating-point ratio, floor, 9-character name 2 edits gives 2/9 = 0.222, exceeds 0.2 rejected.
code: normalized_distance = raw_edits / max(nchar(input), nchar(candidate))

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: fuzzy_threshold integer (1, 2, 3, …), acts absolute cap raw edit count, regardless name length. fuzzy_threshold = 2L means: 2 edits, whether name 5 characters 25 characters long. mode useful know kind errors data. input comes OCR pipeline occasionally drops doubles single character, fuzzy_threshold = 1L captures errors without -matching longer names. Integer thresholds supported Jaro-Winkler, method count discrete edits.
code: # Allow exactly 1 edit, regardless of name length result <- taxify(   c("Qurecus robur", "Achillea milefolium", "Poa anua"),   fuzzy_threshold = 1L ) # "Qurecus robur" matches (1 transposition) # "Achillea milefolium" matches (1 deletion: ll -> l) # "Poa anua" matches (1 deletion: nn -> n)

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: distance computation, taxify runs cleaning pipeline input names. pipeline strips qualifiers (cf., aff., s.l., s.str.), removes authorship strings (L., (Aiton) Sm.), drops brackets trailing numbers, collapses whitespace, lowercases everything except genus. backbone names already clean, step brings user input format. Cleaning aggressive enough many names look like need fuzzy matching actually resolve exact match noise stripped. pipeline also handles Latin orthographic normalization separate exact matching pass. Alternations like ae/(hirtaeformis vs hirtiformis), ph/f, rh/r, th/t, ii/word endings normalized comparison. fuzzy matches; appear exact_ci output. Hybrid markers (multiplication sign standalone “x”) detected stripped cleaning. name like Quercus × hispanica cleaned Quercus hispanica matching, is_hybrid column set TRUE. upshot: fuzzy matching runs names survived cleaning failed exact matching passes (case-sensitive, case-insensitive, Latin normalization, infraspecific--species fallback). time fuzzy matching activates, remaining names genuinely character-level errors.
code: # All three resolve to the same clean form: "Quercus robur" result <- taxify(c(   "Quercus robur L.",   "Quercus robur (L.) Sm.",   "  Quercus  robur  " )) # match_type will be "exact" for all three (no fuzzy needed)

what: Worked example 1: clean names that need no fuzzy matching
title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: well-curated species list, possibly authorship strings attached, typically resolve entirely exact match. Fuzzy matching runs finds nothing . Adding authorship change picture. cleaning pipeline strips matching. message straightforward: curated data, fuzzy matching adds value can safely disabled fuzzy = FALSE skip step entirely. saves small amount time large lists.
code: clean_names <- c(   "Quercus robur",   "Pinus sylvestris",   "Betula pendula",   "Fagus sylvatica",   "Acer pseudoplatanus" ) result <- taxify(clean_names)  # All rows have match_type == "exact" table(result$match_type) # exact #     5  # fuzzy_dist is NA for all rows all(is.na(result$fuzzy_dist)) # TRUE with_authors <- c(   "Quercus robur L.",   "Pinus sylvestris L.",   "Betula pendula Roth",   "Fagus sylvatica L.",   "Acer pseudoplatanus L." ) result <- taxify(with_authors) table(result$match_type) # exact #     5

what: Worked example 2: OCR-degraded and hand-typed names
title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Real-world species lists often arrive typos, especially transcribed handwritten field notes extracted scanned PDFs via OCR. names fuzzy matching designed rescue. transposition errors (Qurecus, officianle, lanceoalata) cost 1 edit Damerau-Levenshtein, producing fuzzy_dist values around 0.07-0.08 13-20 character names. deletion millefolum (missing ) also costs 1 edit. Ranunculus acris exact-matches fuzzy_dist = NA. Consider arithmetic Taraxacum officianle (20 characters). intended target Taraxacum officinale, differs transposition l positions 18-19. 1 edit, giving normalized distance 1 / 20 = 0.05. falls well within 0.2 threshold. Even conservative threshold 0.1 accept . name Lolium perrene (14 characters) extra r compared Lolium perenne, costing 1 insertion, normalized distance 1 / 14 = 0.071. fall comfortably within default threshold 0.2. data error profile, default settings work well box. fuzzy_dist values cluster tightly around 0.05-0.08, giving us high confidence every match correct.
code: messy_names <- c(   "Qurecus robur",         # transposition: ur -> ru   "Taraxacum officianle",  # transposition: al -> la   "Plantago lanceoalata",  # transposition: la -> al   "Trifolium repnes",      # transposition: en -> ne   "Dactylis gloemrata",    # transposition: me -> em   "Lolium perrene",        # insertion: extra r   "Achillea millefolum",   # deletion: i missing   "Ranunculus acris"       # correct (should exact-match) ) result <- taxify(messy_names)  # Check what matched and how result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "match_type", "fuzzy_dist")]

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: loose threshold can match names wrong species. primary risk fuzzy matching, tends bite hardest short names names species-dense genera. threshold 0.4, Poa trialis (10 characters) allowed 4 edits. enough distance reach Poa trivialis (intended target, 2 edits) potentially Poa species happen closer string distance. 500+ Poa species WFO, risk false match real. fix simple: tighten threshold. Names fail fuzzy matching get match_type = "none". unmatched name always better wrong match, can review unmatched names manually. wrong match silent propagates downstream analyses. example also illustrates short names large genera hardest case fuzzy matching. genus Poa 500 accepted species WFO, many epithets differ 2-3 characters (pratensis vs palustris, trivialis vs trivialis). shorter name, fewer edits takes reach threshold, candidate species fall within range. genera like Carex (2,000+ species), Astragalus (3,000+), Euphorbia (2,000+), problem applies. working species-dense genera, tightening threshold 0.1-0.15 almost always right move.
code: # Poa is a large genus with many similar epithets poa_names <- c(   "Poa anua",       # intended: Poa annua (1 edit)   "Poa pratenss",   # intended: Poa pratensis (1 edit)   "Poa trialis"     # intended: Poa trivialis (2 edits) )  # With a loose threshold, some may match the wrong species loose <- taxify(poa_names, fuzzy_threshold = 0.4) loose[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "fuzzy_dist")] tight <- taxify(poa_names, fuzzy_threshold = 0.15) tight[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "match_type", "fuzzy_dist")] # "Poa anua" still matches (1/9 = 0.11 < 0.15) # "Poa pratenss" still matches (1/12 = 0.08 < 0.15) # "Poa trialis" may fail (2/11 = 0.18 > 0.15), safer to leave unmatched

what: Worked example 4: comparing all three methods
title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: input list can produce different results depending distance method used. differences visible errors include transpositions. transposition like Qurecus Quercus, Damerau-Levenshtein reports 1 edit (distance ~0.08 13-character name). Levenshtein reports 2 edits (distance ~0.15). fall within default 0.2 threshold, methods match , Levenshtein distance nearly double. deletions like milefolium millefolium, methods report distance (1 edit), transposition involved. Jaro-Winkler distances tend smaller overall algorithm rewards matching prefixes. name shares entire genus prefix candidate starts high base similarity. practical consequence Jaro-Winkler permissive numeric threshold. threshold 0.2 Jaro-Winkler quite loose; 0.1 comparable starting point. table shows approximate distances errors across three methods, assuming names 13-20 characters: Levenshtein column always equal larger Damerau-Levenshtein column, Levenshtein charges double transpositions. Jaro-Winkler consistently smallest, shared genus prefix dominates similarity calculation. numbers explain threshold value behaves differently across methods need recalibrate switching.
code: test_names <- c(   "Qurecus robur",        # transposition in genus   "Achillea milefolium",  # deletion (l dropped)   "Plantago lanceoalata", # transposition in epithet   "Betula pednula",       # transposition in epithet   "Fagus sylvatcia"       # transposition in epithet )  dl_result  <- taxify(test_names, fuzzy_method = "dl") lev_result <- taxify(test_names, fuzzy_method = "levenshtein") jw_result  <- taxify(test_names, fuzzy_method = "jw")  # Compare fuzzy_dist across methods comparison <- data.frame(   input = test_names,   dl_dist  = dl_result$fuzzy_dist,   lev_dist = lev_result$fuzzy_dist,   jw_dist  = jw_result$fuzzy_dist,   dl_match  = dl_result$match_type,   lev_match = lev_result$match_type,   jw_match  = jw_result$match_type ) comparison

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Every row taxify output fuzzy_dist column. exact matches (including case-insensitive Latin normalization), NA. fuzzy matches, contains normalized distance: number 0 1 lower means closer. column primary tool quality control fuzzy matching. simple filter separates high-confidence matches questionable ones. fuzzy_dist 0.1 name 10+ characters means 1 edit . almost always correct. fuzzy_dist 0.1 0.2 means 1-3 edits depending name length, warrants glance. Anything 0.15 short name (10 characters) deserves scrutiny. systematic review, sorting fuzzy_dist descending order puts suspect matches top. practice, datasets bimodal distribution fuzzy_dist: peak near 0.05-0.08 (single typos medium-length names) sparse tail 0.12 (multiple errors short names one error). tail false matches hide. useful rule thumb: 5% fuzzy matches fuzzy_dist 0.15, threshold probably loose dataset. Either tighten , keep current threshold flag matches 0.12 manual review. cost reviewing dozen names small compared cost propagating wrong species identity trait analysis distribution model.
code: result <- taxify(my_species_list)  # High-confidence fuzzy matches (likely just typos) good_fuzzy <- result[result$match_type == "fuzzy" &                      result$fuzzy_dist < 0.1, ]  # Questionable fuzzy matches (review manually) check_fuzzy <- result[result$match_type == "fuzzy" &                       result$fuzzy_dist >= 0.1, ] fuzzy_rows <- result[result$match_type == "fuzzy", ] fuzzy_rows <- fuzzy_rows[order(-fuzzy_rows$fuzzy_dist), ] head(fuzzy_rows[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "fuzzy_dist")], 20)

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: default, fuzzy matching genus-blocked: taxify extracts genus input name compares backbone entries genus. fast (avoids comparing every input millions candidates) reduces false matches (misspelled epithet accidentally match completely different genus). downside misspelled genus produces match , backbone entries share misspelled genus. taxify handles second pass: genus-blocked fuzzy matching, names still unmatched run prefix-blocked fuzzy join. pass blocks first two characters name rather full genus. genus typos preserve first two characters (Qurecus still starts Qu, Betual still starts ), prefix block catches still pruning search space substantially. two-pass strategy means genus typos handled automatically. nothing configure. case misses typo first two characters genus, rare enough practice accept trade-. One consequence worth knowing: name misspelled genus higher fuzzy_dist name epithet typo, genus error adds edits top epithet error. input Qeurcus robru (two errors: genus transposition + epithet transposition), total edit count 2, giving normalized distance 2 / 13 = 0.154. still falls within default threshold, lands zone manual review advisable.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: curated checklists, validated databases, input already name-resolution service, fuzzy matching adds risk without benefit. Disable . also makes call faster, fuzzy join step skipped entirely. list 100,000 names, difference can several seconds.
code: result <- taxify(curated_list, fuzzy = FALSE)

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Tighten default 0.2 input names short (many 2-word names 12 characters), genera species-rich (Carex, Poa, Astragalus, Euphorbia), false matches costly (conservation assessments, regulatory lists). threshold 0.1 good conservative choice. still catches single-character typos names 10+ characters rejects matches require 2+ edits shorter names.
code: result <- taxify(short_grass_list, fuzzy_threshold = 0.1)

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Loosen default 0.2 input comes OCR degraded documents, names transliterated across character encodings, completeness matters precision (initial screening pass unmatched names expensive follow ). threshold 0.25-0.3 reasonable OCR data; going 0.3 rarely justified.
code: result <- taxify(ocr_names, fuzzy_threshold = 0.25) # Then filter questionable matches: suspect <- result[result$fuzzy_dist > 0.15, ]

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Stick Damerau-Levenshtein ("dl") unless specific reason change. Switch Levenshtein ("levenshtein") controlled data transpositions unlikely want stricter distance. Switch Jaro-Winkler ("jw") short names (3-6 characters, e.g., matching genus level) prefix weighting helps, remember lower threshold 0.1 .

what: Using integer thresholds for uniform error budgets
title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: error model known (e.g., “OCR pipeline drops adds 1 character”), integer thresholds give direct control. fuzzy_threshold = 1L means exactly says: 1 edit, name length. avoids length-dependent behavior fractional thresholds 5-character name gets 1 edit 25-character name gets 5 edits. Integer thresholds available Jaro-Winkler. Passing integer fuzzy_method = "jw" raises error.
code: # Uniform 2-edit budget, regardless of name length result <- taxify(my_names, fuzzy_threshold = 2L)

what: A two-pass workflow for messy data
title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: datasets unknown error rates (historical collections, aggregated multi-source lists), two-pass approach gives best worlds. First pass: run tight threshold fuzzy = TRUE get high-confidence matches. Second pass: extract unmatched names, run looser threshold, review additional fuzzy matches manually. avoids --nothing choice tight loose thresholds. bulk data gets matched high confidence, residual names get looser treatment explicit human oversight.
code: # Pass 1: conservative pass1 <- taxify(my_names, fuzzy_threshold = 0.1) unmatched <- pass1$input_name[pass1$match_type == "none"]  # Pass 2: permissive, for manual review pass2 <- taxify(unmatched, fuzzy_threshold = 0.25) needs_review <- pass2[pass2$match_type == "fuzzy", ] needs_review[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "fuzzy_dist")]

what: Summary of output columns related to fuzzy matching
title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: match_type fuzzy_dist columns together give complete picture match quality. Exact matches definitive, fuzzy matches distance 0.05 near-certain corrections minor typos. distance climbs toward 0.15 , manual review becomes worthwhile matched name may belong different species entirely.

title: Hybrid name detection and parsing
text: Botanical nomenclature uses dedicated marker hybrids: multiplication sign (×, U+00D7). marker appears three distinct positions, signalling different kind hybrid. nothogenus places marker genus name, signalling intergeneric hybrid (cross species two different genera). Leyland cypress well-known example: ×Cupressocyparis leylandii nothospecies places marker specific epithet, genus sides cross: Mentha ×piperita Peppermint (Mentha ×piperita, cross M. aquatica M. spicata) classic case. third form, hybrid formula, names parent species explicitly, joined multiplication sign: Salix alba × Salix fragilis real-world data, multiplication sign frequently replaced lowercase uppercase “x”. Herbarium databases, spreadsheet exports, OCR outputs rarely preserve Unicode character. taxify accepts three forms (×, x, X) normalizes internally. detection logic distinguishes standalone “x” used hybrid marker “x” part word (e.g., genus Saxifraga) requiring whitespace boundaries around letter.
code: library(taxify)

title: Hybrid name detection and parsing
text: Detection happens early pipeline, name cleaning backbone matching. taxify() receives input vector, name passes clean_names(), calls internal detect_hybrid() function. function tokenizes name, looks hybrid marker specific positions, classifies result nothogenus, nothospecies, formula, non-hybrid. output taxify() includes is_hybrid column (logical) records whether hybrid marker found original input. column always present regardless whether name ultimately matched backbone record. finer classification nothogenus, nothospecies, formula exposed directly main output; becomes available add_hybrid_info(), cover looking matched hybrids behave result table. detection, hybrid marker stripped name matching. nothospecies like “Mentha ×piperita”, cleaned form becomes “Mentha piperita”. hybrid formula like “Salix alba × Salix fragilis”, first parent binomial (“Salix alba”) retained cleaned name, since formulas single taxon names match backbone record directly. nothospecies, taxify also constructs secondary search form multiplication sign reinserted (“Mentha × piperita”) attempts match backbone. backbones store nothospecies × character canonical name, secondary attempt can recover matches stripped form misses.

what: Worked example: matching a mixed species list
title: Hybrid name detection and parsing
text: Consider list includes ordinary species, nothospecies, nothogenus, hybrid formula. pass taxify() single call. expected output looks roughly like : Several things visible . two nothospecies (Mentha, Platanus) matched successfully WFO stores accepted names × character canonical name. nothogenus ×Cupressocyparis returned match intergeneric hybrid genera less commonly included backbone databases. hybrid formula matched first parent (Salix alba), since formula single taxon name. is_hybrid column TRUE four hybrid inputs, regardless whether name matched. column records property input, match result.

title: Hybrid name detection and parsing
text: Hybrid formulas appear botanical horticultural datasets often one might expect. Field botanists record parentage specimen known suspected. formulas vary notation: spell genera full, others abbreviate second genus. genus abbreviation “Q.” second example expanded “Quercus” automatically. taxify infers full genus first parent formula. expansion happened “R.” “Rosa” fourth row. expansion purely textual: first token first parent used genus second parent whenever second parent’s genus field matches pattern single capital letter followed period.
code: formulas <- c(   "Salix alba x Salix fragilis",   "Quercus pyrenaica x Q. petraea",   "Populus nigra x Populus deltoides",   "Rosa canina x R. gallica" )  formula_result <- taxify(formulas, backend = "wfo") formula_result <- formula_result |> add_hybrid_info()  formula_result[, c("input_name", "hybrid_type",                     "hybrid_parent_1", "hybrid_parent_2")]

what: What matches and what does not
title: Hybrid name detection and parsing
text: three hybrid types different matching profiles backbone databases. Nothospecies best-supported form. WFO COL store many nothospecies accepted names, × character part canonical name. Mentha ×piperita, Platanus ×hispanica, Narcissus ×medioluteus examples appear backbones. taxify’s matching logic handles marker correctly: first tries stripped form (“Mentha piperita”) form × reinserted (“Mentha × piperita”). least one typically matches. Nothogenera lower coverage. Intergeneric hybrids like ×Cupressocyparis, ×Triticosecale, ×Festulolium exist backbones absent others. WFO includes several nothogenera relevant agriculture horticulture. COL’s coverage varies taxonomic group. nothogenus match, output row match_type = "none" accepted_name = NA, is_hybrid still TRUE. Hybrid formulas match backbone record directly, formula taxon name. taxify extracts first parent binomial cleaned name matching, result row reflects match status first parent. resolve parents, match separately. approach gives full match result (accepted name, synonym status, authorship) parent individually. dataset many hybrid formulas, can extract parent columns add_hybrid_info() feed back taxify() batch.
code: # Match both parents of a hybrid formula separately parents <- c("Salix alba", "Salix fragilis") parent_result <- taxify(parents, backend = "wfo") # Batch-resolve all hybrid formula parents info <- result |> add_hybrid_info() formula_rows <- info[info$hybrid_type == "formula" & !is.na(info$hybrid_type), ]  all_parents <- unique(na.omit(c(   formula_rows$hybrid_parent_1,   formula_rows$hybrid_parent_2 )))  parent_matches <- taxify(all_parents, backend = "wfo")

what: The multiplication sign and its substitutes
title: Hybrid name detection and parsing
text: Unicode multiplication sign (U+00D7) correct character hybrid notation International Code Nomenclature. practice, data arrive three common representations: Unicode character : × (common well-curated databases) lowercase x surrounded spaces (common spreadsheets field data) uppercase X surrounded spaces (less common, occurs older databases OCR output) taxify normalizes three forms internally. detect_hybrid() function replaces every occurrence U+00D7 space-padded “x” works uniform token stream, downstream logic needs handle one representation. space-boundary requirement prevents false positives: “Saxifraga” trigger hybrid detection “x” sits within word rather standing alone tokens. subtlety arises mojibake. UTF-8 text containing × character read Latin-1 Windows-1252 encoding, two-byte sequence can misinterpreted “0c3097” “0c3014”. name cleaning pipeline detects repairs common misreadings hybrid detection runs, names corrupted encoding errors still handled correctly.

title: Inspecting a name list with inspect()
text: default inspect() match anything. plain character vector runs checks need backbone: asks genus register whether genus real one, compares name rest batch. fast offline. Three names surface. Bogusia fakensis uses genus backbone recognises, reads real name. Festuca rubraa one letter spelling appears twice list, mark typo . code: names <- c(   "Quercus robur",   "Panthera leo",         # an animal among plants   "Bogusia fakensis",     # not a real genus   "Festuca rubra",   "Festuca rubra",   "Festuca rubraa",       # one stray letter   "Pinus sylvestris",   "Pinus abies"           # a synonym of Picea abies )  inspect(names) #> ── taxify inspection ────────────────────────────────────── #>   8 names inspected  |  3 with anomalies #>   backbones: none (register + list checks only) #>   unresolved: 1   review: 2 #>   ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   [unresolved] Bogusia fakensis  ->  ?               genus 'Bogusia' is not in the taxonomic register #>   [review    ] Festuca rubraa    ->  Festuca rubra   near-duplicate of more frequent 'Festuca rubra' #>   [review    ] Panthera leo      ->  ?               animalia outlier (list is mostly plantae)

title: Inspecting a name list with inspect()
text: pick typos, synonyms, ambiguity, let inspect() match. simplest route backbones = TRUE, runs names every installed backbone records ones used report header. Now Pinus abies recognised synonym resolved Picea abies, Festuca rubraa carries list-context label fuzzy typo label confirms . Panthera leo now matches GBIF, longer candidate typo, remains kingdom-group outlier plant list. already matched list, inspect result instead asking inspect() match . reuses exact backend, region, options original call. two routes return kind report. Pass backbones = TRUE want quick standalone check; pipe result matching already part workflow.
code: inspect(names, backbones = TRUE) #> ── taxify inspection ────────────────────────────────────── #>   8 names inspected  |  4 with anomalies #>   backbones: WFO, GBIF #>   unresolved: 1   review: 2   note: 1 #>   ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   [unresolved] Bogusia fakensis  ->  ?              genus 'Bogusia' is not in the taxonomic register #>   [review    ] Festuca rubraa    ->  Festuca rubra  likely misspelling; near-duplicate of more frequent 'Festuca rubra' #>   [review    ] Panthera leo      ->  Panthera leo   animalia outlier (list is mostly plantae) #>   [note      ] Pinus abies       ->  Picea abies    outdated synonym taxify(names, backend = c("wfo", "gbif")) |>   inspect()

title: Working with large species lists
text: Every backbone ships .vtr file: binary columnar format written vectra C11 engine. Unlike CSV TSV, .vtr format stores column contiguously disk lightweight compression. taxify never parses text query time. read.csv() step, string splitting, quote escaping. backbone already query-ready binary layout. Backbones distributed pre-built .vtr files (hosted Zenodo GitHub Releases), users download single binary file ready query immediately — CSV parsing, conversion step. .vtr files typically 30-50% smaller original Darwin Core CSV columnar layout compresses string columns efficiently row-oriented text.

title: Working with large species lists
text: backbone first used session, vectra materializes -memory columnar block hash indexes name genus columns. Subsequent lookups block run essentially constant time per name. Exact matching uses block_lookup(), resolves input name via hash index. O(1) operation per name reason exact matching scales linearly list size. list 100,000 clean plant names matches WFO seconds, minutes. exact pipeline thorough simple string comparison. runs five passes sequence, catching different class name variation: Case-sensitive exact match canonical name column. Case-insensitive match precomputed lowercased key. Latin orthographic normalization maps common epithet variants (e.g., -ii -, -anum -ana) canonical form. Infraspecific--species fallback strips variety/subspecies qualifiers matches binomial. Hybrid name normalization resolves nothospecies formatting differences (e.g., Salix x rubens vs. Salix xrubens). five passes use hash lookups. name matches pass 1 never tested passes 2-5. practice, pass 1 resolves 85-95% names clean input, passes 2-4 pick another 2-5%. total cost exact matching dominated hash lookups, O(1) per name regardless backbone size.
