Large genera like Carex (~2,000 entries WFO) Astragalus (~3,000 entries) expensive per name small genera. cost grows number names fail exact matching size backbone. GBIF’s 7 million rows, 5,000 names might take 30-90 seconds genus block proportionally larger. secondary fuzzy pass handles misspelled genera. genus wrong (e.g., Qurecus instead Quercus), genus-blocked join misses name entirely. taxify runs fallback pass blocks first two characters name instead full genus. catches single- character genus typos keeping search space much smaller full cross-join. practical consequence: exact-matching fast scale, fuzzy matching knob controls long run takes.

what: Backbone loading and the session cache
title: Working with large species lists
text: first time taxify() called given backend, several things happen behind scenes. function resolves backbone path four-step fallback: session cache, versioned directory disk, legacy flat directory, finally auto-download Zenodo local copy exists. path known, vectra materializes .vtr -memory columnar block builds hash indexes name genus columns. initialization step takes 1-3 seconds WFO (~400,000 rows) 5-10 seconds GBIF (~7 million rows). Every subsequent taxify() call R session reuses materialized block. repeated file /O. Two caches operate parallel. path cache (.taxify_cache) maps backend names .vtr file paths disk. path resolved, stays cached ensure_backbone() re-scan file system. data cache (.taxify_env) holds materialized columnar block , keyed file path. also stores session manifest, version-check flags, enrichment paths, coverage data genus register. package-level environments persist duration R session shared across taxify() calls. first taxify() call session also triggers version check. taxify fetches manifest GitHub (small JSON file listing latest version backbone) compares locally installed version. newer backbone available, downloaded automatically. check runs per backend per session. Subsequent calls skip entirely. network unavailable, check skipped local copy used -. see backbones live disk: Linux typically ~/.local/share/R/taxify, macOS ~/Library/Application Support/R/taxify. path determined tools::R_user_dir("taxify", "data") shared across R projects machine. Two R sessions running machine can read backbone files concurrently without conflict .vtr files read-query time.
code: taxify_data_dir() #> [1] "C:/Users/jane/AppData/Local/R/taxify"

what: Worked example: exact vs. fuzzy matching
title: Working with large species lists
text: simplest performance lever fuzzy argument. input names clean (e.g., names curated database, existing taxonomic checklist, output previous taxify run), disabling fuzzy matching skips string-distance computation entirely. Consider list 10,000 plant names extracted herbarium database names already standard binomial form. time modes: exact-run order magnitude faster. exact pass matches names first try five-pass pipeline (case-sensitive, case-insensitive, Latin orthographic normalization, infraspecific fallback, hybrid normalization). Fuzzy matching picks remaining names minor misspellings, cost scales number unmatched names times average genus size backbone. ratio two modes depends input quality. list names extracted curated database (GBIF occurrence records, published checklist, previous taxify run), exact pass resolves 95-99% names fuzzy pass adds little. OCR-transcribed herbarium labels citizen science data frequent misspellings, exact pass might resolve 70-80% fuzzy pass becomes essential. practical two-pass pattern large lists: run exact-first, inspect unmatched names, decide whether fuzzy pass worth time. pattern especially useful 1-5% names need fuzzy matching. exact pass finishes seconds even 100,000 names, fuzzy pass operates much smaller subset. total wall time often less half single fuzzy = TRUE call take, fuzzy engine need allocate working memory build query tables names already matched exactly. One subtlety: second call taxify() re-materialize backbone. session cache first call still active, fuzzy-pass starts immediately string-distance computation. penalty splitting work two calls.
code: # Assume `species_list` is a character vector of 10,000 plant names  # Exact + fuzzy (default) t_fuzzy <- system.time(   result_fuzzy <- taxify(species_list, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = TRUE) )  # Exact only t_exact <- system.time(   result_exact <- taxify(species_list, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = FALSE) )  t_fuzzy["elapsed"] #> elapsed #>   18.4  t_exact["elapsed"] #> elapsed #>    2.1 # Pass 1: exact only result <- taxify(species_list, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = FALSE)  # How many names remain unmatched? n_unmatched <- sum(result$match_type == "none") message(n_unmatched, " names unmatched after exact pass")  # Pass 2: fuzzy only on the unmatched subset if (n_unmatched > 0) {   unmatched_names <- result$input_name[result$match_type == "none"]   fuzzy_result <- taxify(unmatched_names, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = TRUE)    # Merge back   matched_rows <- fuzzy_result$match_type != "none"   idx <- match(fuzzy_result$input_name[matched_rows],                result$input_name)   result[idx, ] <- fuzzy_result[matched_rows, ] }

title: Working with large species lists
text: taxify() receives multiple backends, processes sequential fallback chain. Names matched earlier backend excluded later ones. order matters performance: first backend sees names, second sees failed, . Suppose mixed-kingdom species list freshwater ecology survey: mostly aquatic plants, fish, handful invertebrates. WFO covers plants, COL covers everything larger slower search. Putting WFO first means plant names (majority) resolved quickly, animal names fall COL. results identical terms name resolution (backends ultimately resolve names accepted names), WFO-first ordering faster WFO’s smaller backbone (~400,000 rows) resolves 75% list COL’s larger backbone (~4.5 million rows) ever touched. saving comes two sources: exact pass WFO faster (smaller hash table), fuzzy pass COL runs 2,000 names instead 8,000. Since fuzzy matching cost scales linearly number unmatched names, resolving 6,000 names via WFO’s fast exact pass eliminates need 6,000 fuzzy comparisons COL’s much larger genus blocks. Note backend column output records backend resolved name. useful quality control: name resolved second backend chain, means first backend either contain matched differently. Inspecting backend column multi-backend run can reveal patterns taxonomic coverage gaps. General guidelines backend ordering: Plant-lists: "wfo" alone sufficient. WFO complete plant synonym coverage compact backbone. Marine lists: "worms" first, "col" "gbif" anything WoRMS misses. Mixed-kingdom lists: put backbone covers dominant kingdom first. list 80% plants 20% animals, c("wfo", "col") faster c("col", "wfo"). Maximizing coverage: c("wfo", "col", "gbif") casts widest net involves three backbone loads. lists 10,000 names extra loading time negligible. 100,000+ names, extra fuzzy passes add .
code: # 8,000 names: ~6,000 plants, ~1,500 fish, ~500 invertebrates t_wfo_first <- system.time(   result_a <- taxify(survey_names,                      backend = c("wfo", "col"),                      fuzzy = TRUE) ) t_wfo_first["elapsed"] #> elapsed #>   25.3  # Reversed order: COL first, WFO second t_col_first <- system.time(   result_b <- taxify(survey_names,                      backend = c("col", "wfo"),                      fuzzy = TRUE) ) t_col_first["elapsed"] #> elapsed #>   41.7

what: Worked example: batch processing a very large list
title: Working with large species lists
text: Lists 100,000 names common biodiversity informatics. national herbarium digitization project might produce 500,000 label transcriptions. metabarcoding pipeline might output 200,000 OTU labels. Processing single taxify() call works, splitting chunks gives two practical benefits: progress monitoring memory stability. taxify’s matching engine handles full vector internally, single taxify() call 500,000 names produce correct results. two practical issues arise scale. First, fuzzy-join working set grows input size: 500,000 names 10% unmatched means 50,000 fuzzy comparisons, scanning genus block. temporary .vtr files distance matrices many comparisons can spike memory several hundred MB. Second, R process interrupted mid-run (Ctrl+C, OOM kill, session timeout), entire result lost. Chunking 50,000-100,000 names keeps peak memory predictable provides natural restart points. backbone stays memory across chunks (session cache cleared calls), chunk first skips initialization overhead. fuzzy-join working set allocated freed per chunk. lists millions (e.g., processing occurrence records GBIF download), consider writing results disk chunk rather accumulating memory: pattern keeps R’s memory usage bounded single chunk regardless total list size. also makes workflow resumable: process dies chunk 47 60, can check .rds files exist restart chunk 48. Adding simple skip condition handles : One detail worth noting: chunk boundaries arbitrary affect matching quality. chunk matched independently backbone. name appears chunk 3 gets result appeared chunk 7 backbone deterministic matching logic stateless across calls. shared state chunks session cache (materialized backbone block), improves performance avoiding repeated initialization.
code: # 300,000 names from a herbarium digitization project all_names <- readLines("herbarium_names.txt") chunk_size <- 50000  # Split into chunks chunks <- split(all_names,                 ceiling(seq_along(all_names) / chunk_size))  # Process each chunk results <- lapply(seq_along(chunks), function(i) {   message(sprintf("Chunk %d/%d (%d names)...",                   i, length(chunks), length(chunks[[i]])))   taxify(chunks[[i]], backend = "wfo", fuzzy = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) })  # Combine result <- do.call(rbind, results) nrow(result) #> [1] 300000 output_dir <- "results" dir.create(output_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)  for (i in seq_along(chunks)) {   message(sprintf("Chunk %d/%d", i, length(chunks)))   res <- taxify(chunks[[i]], backend = "wfo",                 fuzzy = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)   saveRDS(res, file.path(output_dir,                          sprintf("chunk_%04d.rds", i))) }  # Combine when needed all_files <- list.files(output_dir, pattern = "\\.rds$",                         full.names = TRUE) result <- do.call(rbind, lapply(all_files, readRDS)) for (i in seq_along(chunks)) {   out_file <- file.path(output_dir, sprintf("chunk_%04d.rds", i))   if (file.exists(out_file)) next   message(sprintf("Chunk %d/%d", i, length(chunks)))   res <- taxify(chunks[[i]], backend = "wfo",                 fuzzy = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)   saveRDS(res, out_file) }

title: Working with large species lists
text: taxify uses two internal environments session-level caching. path cache stores disk location loaded backbone. data cache stores materialized columnar blocks, session manifest, version-check flags, enrichment paths. persist R session ends user explicitly clears . taxify_clear_cache() removes loaded backbone paths memory. next taxify() call re-read disk re-materialize. useful large matching run backbone longer needed memory can reclaimed. Clearing cache delete files disk. .vtr files remain taxify_data_dir() reloaded next use. cost reloading 1-10 second initialization time first call session incurs. workflow matching done one phase downstream modelling another, worthwhile trade: spend 3 seconds reloading WFO later needed, free 100 MB RAM memory-intensive ordination species distribution model. taxify_refresh_manifest() narrower operation: invalidates cached copy remote manifest (JSON file listing latest version backbone enrichment). forces next taxify() call re-check updates. Normally manifest fetched per session cached. long-running R session (e.g., RStudio session stays open days), calling taxify_refresh_manifest() batch run ensures working latest backbone version. new backbone release published since session started, version check detect trigger automatic download.
code: # After finishing all matching work taxify_clear_cache() gc() taxify_refresh_manifest()

what: Disk storage and sharing across projects
title: Working with large species lists
text: taxify data lives taxify_data_dir(), resolves platform-specific user data directory via tools::R_user_dir("taxify", "data"). layout : directory per-user, per-project. backbone downloaded available every R project machine without duplication. need copy .vtr files project directory version-control . multiple users shared server need backbones, one user can download others can set R_USER_DATA_DIR environment variable (symlink taxify_data_dir()) shared location. .vtr files read-query time, concurrent access multiple R sessions safe. file locking needed. check much disk space taxify currently using: remove specific backbone (e.g., GBIF finishing project needed ), delete directory: Deleting backbone directory safe. next taxify() call backend re-download Zenodo needed.
code: taxify_data_dir()/   wfo/     latest/       wfo.vtr          # the backbone       wfo.meta         # download provenance       meta.json        # version metadata   col/     latest/       col.vtr       ...   enrichment/     conservation_status/       latest/         conservation_status.vtr         meta.json     woodiness/       latest/         woodiness.vtr         meta.json     ... # Total size of all backbones and enrichments data_dir <- taxify_data_dir() files <- list.files(data_dir, recursive = TRUE, full.names = TRUE) total_mb <- sum(file.size(files), na.rm = TRUE) / 1048576 message(sprintf("taxify data: %.0f MB across %d files",                 total_mb, length(files))) # Remove GBIF backbone (frees ~500-700 MB) unlink(file.path(taxify_data_dir(), "gbif"), recursive = TRUE)  # Clear the session cache so taxify() doesn't try to use the old path taxify_clear_cache()

title: Working with large species lists
text: reproducible batch pipeline (e.g., Makefile targets plan), cleaner separate download step analysis step. Downloads can fail due network issues, want know 2-hour matching run starts. taxify_download_vtr() downloads one backbone .vtr files. taxify_download_enrichment() enrichment layers. idempotent: file already exists version current, return immediately. CI/CD cluster environment, download step can run setup script container build phase. matching step operates entirely local disk, network dependency risk mid-run download failures. separation also makes pipeline reproducible: download step pins specific backbone version (recorded meta.json sidecar file), matching step uses whatever version disk. targets drake plan, download calls fit naturally upstream targets matching targets depend . return value (.vtr path) can passed dependency graph, though practice path resolved internally ensure_backbone() need passed explicitly. see enrichments available current versions: Static enrichments (based published, version-locked datasets like EltonTraits 1.0 PanTHERIA 1.0) never re-downloaded initial fetch. Non-static enrichments (conservation_status, griis, wcvp, common_names) checked per session updated newer build available.
code: # Pre-download everything needed for a multi-kingdom analysis # with conservation status and trait enrichments  # Backbones taxify_download_vtr(c("wfo", "col"))  # Enrichments taxify_download_enrichment(c(   "conservation_status",   "woodiness",   "eive",   "elton_traits" ))  # Now the analysis can run fully offline result <- taxify(species_list, backend = c("wfo", "col")) result <- add_conservation_status(result) result <- add_woodiness(result) list_enrichments() #>                name version    nrow static #> 1 conservation_status 2026.04   59583  FALSE #> 2               griis 2026.04   98131  FALSE #> 3                wcvp 2026.04 1973234  FALSE #> 4                eive     1.0   14835   TRUE #> 5        elton_traits     1.0   15394   TRUE #> 6              avonet     1.0   11009   TRUE #> ...

title: Working with large species lists
text: table summarizes recommended settings list size. guidelines, hard thresholds. actual performance depends input cleanliness (many names need fuzzy matching), backbone size (WFO vs. GBIF), hardware (number cores, available RAM, disk speed). 1,000 names. defaults work well. taxify(names) fuzzy = TRUE single backend completes seconds. tuning needed. regime interactive analysis: field survey, thesis species list, table extracted paper. Memory usage negligible. 1,000 50,000 names. input clean (names curated database, previous taxify run, standard checklist), consider fuzzy = FALSE. exact pipeline handles case differences, Latin orthographic variants (e.g., -ii vs. -endings), infraspecific-- species fallback without string-distance computation. Enabling fuzzy clean list 50,000 names might add 30-60 seconds practical gain. input known quality issues (OCR transcriptions, citizen science data), leave fuzzy expect 1-3 minutes WFO. 50,000 500,000 names. Backend ordering starts matter. Put backbone covers dominant taxon group first. plant list, "wfo" alone suffices. mixed-kingdom lists, c("wfo", "col") resolves names faster WFO pass. Consider two-pass pattern (exact first, fuzzy unmatched) small fraction names quality issues. GBIF backbone ~7 million rows expensive fuzzy matching; avoid first backend unless list primarily non-plant, non-marine taxa covered COL. 500,000 names. Batch chunks 50,000-100,000 names. backbone stays cached across chunks, repeated initialization cost. Write results disk per chunk total memory concern. Clear cache analysis phases (matching, enrichment, downstream modelling) keep memory usage bounded. enriching multiple layers, apply enrichments chunk writing rather accumulating full result memory enriching . Backend selection cheat sheet: single-kingdom list, starting specialist backbone (WFO plants, WoRMS marine, NCBI molecular) falling back COL GBIF gives best balance speed coverage. specialist backbone resolves names quickly (smaller backbone, faster exact pass), generalist backbone catches remainder.
code: # Pattern for 500,000+ names with enrichments for (i in seq_along(chunks)) {   res <- taxify(chunks[[i]], backend = "wfo",                 fuzzy = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)   res <- add_conservation_status(res, verbose = FALSE)   res <- add_woodiness(res, verbose = FALSE)   saveRDS(res, sprintf("results/chunk_%04d.rds", i)) }

title: Working with large species lists
text: default fuzzy threshold 0.2 (normalized Damerau-Levenshtein distance: edits divided maximum two name lengths). threshold 0.2 allows roughly one edit per five characters, catches single-character typos binomials typical length (15-25 characters). good default use cases. large lists noisy input (OCR, handwriting transcription), slightly higher threshold like 0.25 catches misspellings also increases false positives. clean input fuzzy matching serves safety net, lower threshold like 0.1 0.15 reduces risk incorrect matches without sacrificing much recall. threshold also affects performance. higher threshold means backbone entries pass distance filter, means candidate matches evaluate rank. difference modest inputs can noticeable large genera: threshold 0.2, query Astragalus (~3,000 WFO entries) might return 5 candidates; 0.3, might return 20. alternative mode uses integer thresholds. Setting fuzzy_threshold = 2L allows 2 raw edit operations regardless name length. useful long infraspecific names normalized threshold 0.2 might allow many edits. Integer thresholds supported Jaro-Winkler method (fuzzy_method = "jw").
code: # Tight threshold for clean input result <- taxify(clean_names, backend = "wfo",                  fuzzy = TRUE, fuzzy_threshold = 0.1)  # Integer threshold: at most 2 edits, period result <- taxify(noisy_names, backend = "wfo",                  fuzzy = TRUE, fuzzy_threshold = 2L)

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: R ecosystem rich set taxonomic name-resolution tools. takes different design choice along three axes: data lives (local files remote APIs), many backbones bundled, package returns. table summarizes options likely overlap taxify workflow. workflow already uses one happy , urgent reason switch. said, situations taxify offers better fit: Multiple backbones. taxify matches seven backbones offline can chain single call: taxify(names, backend = c("wfo", "col", "gbif")). Speed scale. matching engine written C genus-blocked fuzzy joins. Ten thousand names resolve seconds. Enrichments. Results pipe directly sixty published trait status datasets (IUCN, GRIIS, WCVP, EIVE, EltonTraits, etc.) single |> chain. Reproducibility. Backbones versioned files disk. backbone_version column records exactly snapshot used. vignette maps old APIs taxify equivalents, walks three side--side examples, honest taxify cover.

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: table maps taxize name-resolution functions closest taxify equivalent. taxize also functions serve different purpose (fetching database IDs, enumerating child taxa, retrieving occurrence sequence data). name-resolution functions, taxify cover . “taxify ” section points right packages tasks. key structural difference: taxize returned results varied formats depending function (classification() gave nested list data.frames, synonyms() another nested list, get_tsn() character vector attributes). taxify returns 16-column data.frame every call. Synonym status, classification, match quality columns, separate API calls.

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: WorldFlora returns wide data.frame WFO-specific column names (scientificName, taxonID, taxonomicStatus, acceptedNameUsageID, plus authorship bibliographic fields). taxify normalizes backend-agnostic schema: matched_name, taxon_id, accepted_name, accepted_id, . WFO-specific columns still accessible via add_wfo_info() needed, default output 16 columns whether backend WFO, COL, GBIF. taxify also handles backbone management automatically: first taxify() call downloads backbone, subsequent calls reuse local copy, -per-session version check keeps current.

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: lcvplants wraps Leipzig Catalogue Vascular Plants ships LCVP table bundled data. package centres LCVP() lcvp_search(). LCVP WCVP backbones can combined single fallback chain arbitrate Leipzig Kew vascular-plant authorities:
code: result <- taxify(plant_names, backend = c("wcvp", "lcvp", "wfo")) result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "backend")]

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: rWCVP Kew package World Checklist Vascular Plants. name-resolution side centres wcvp_match_names() wcvp_check_gbif(); also strong distribution-query side taxify replace. rWCVP’s distribution-query functions (wcvp_occ_mat(), generate_checklist()) operate TDWG geography outside taxify’s scope. native-range data joined name-resolved result, add_wcvp() covers common case; full geographic queries, rWCVP remains right tool.

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: taxadb closest functional analogue taxify. store backbone snapshots locally avoid network calls query time. two packages differ matching strategy integration: taxadb returns long-format table exact-key joins, taxify returns flat one-row-per-input result fuzzy matching, synonym resolution, trait enrichment built . two largest practical differences: Matching scope. taxadb built around exact lookups pre-cleaned input. taxify cleans input automatically runs fuzzy matching names match exactly, catches typos, orthographic variants, authorship strings without separate preprocessing step. Output shape. taxadb returns multiple rows per input name multiple matches (pick row want dplyr::filter). taxify returns one row per input best-match selection rule (ACCEPTED SYNONYM, species rank higher ranks, lowest ID tiebreaker), reports match type fuzzy distance columns. workflows already use taxadb’s column-oriented querying custom analyses, taxadb’s approach clean fit. workflows need single resolved name per input plus enrichment joins, taxify’s flat output closer goal.

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: Taxonstand built around Plant List, Kew retired 2013 favour WCVP WFO. package still works, underlying taxonomy updated since retirement. simplest migration replace backend = "tpl" backend = c("wcvp", "wfo") (backend = c("lcvp", "wcvp", "wfo") triple-arbitration across three large vascular-plant authorities).

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: taxize, name resolution typically meant several separate calls: gnr_resolve() matching, get_gbifid() IDs, classification() hierarchy, synonyms() synonym status. taxify, one call: output data.frame 16 columns one row per input name.
code: # --- taxize --- library(taxize)  names <- c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula",            "Panthera leo", "Salmo trutta")  resolved  <- gnr_resolve(names, best_match_only = TRUE) gbif_ids  <- get_gbifid(names) class_list <- classification(gbif_ids, db = "gbif") syn_list   <- synonyms(gbif_ids, db = "gbif") # --- taxify --- library(taxify)  names <- c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula",            "Panthera leo", "Salmo trutta")  result <- taxify(names, backend = "gbif")  result$accepted_name result$family result$genus result$is_synonym result$taxon_id        # GBIF usage key

what: Example 2: WFO matching with fuzzy + synonyms
title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: WorldFlora, typical workflow : load backbone, run match (exact fuzzy together, faster fuzzyjoin engine), collapse candidate rows one best match per input. taxify, exact matching, fuzzy matching, synonym resolution happen single call: Quercus pedonculata misspelling synonym. taxify handles : fuzzy matcher corrects spelling Quercus pedunculata, synonym resolver maps Quercus robur.
code: # --- WorldFlora --- library(WorldFlora)  wfo_data <- read.delim("classification.txt")  names <- c("Quercus robur", "Quercus pedonculata",            "Pinus silvestris", "Rosa canina") matched <- WFO.match.fuzzyjoin(names, WFO.data = wfo_data) best    <- WFO.one(matched) # --- taxify --- library(taxify)  names <- c("Quercus robur", "Quercus pedonculata",            "Pinus silvestris", "Rosa canina")  result <- taxify(names, backend = "wfo")  # Misspellings are caught by fuzzy matching: result[, c("input_name", "matched_name", "match_type", "fuzzy_dist")] #   input_name           matched_name        match_type fuzzy_dist # 1 Quercus robur        Quercus robur       exact              NA # 2 Quercus pedonculata  Quercus pedunculata fuzzy           0.053 # 3 Pinus silvestris     Pinus sylvestris    fuzzy           0.063 # 4 Rosa canina          Rosa canina         exact              NA  # Synonyms resolved automatically: result[, c("input_name", "is_synonym", "accepted_name")]

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: taxify can chain multiple backbones single call. Unmatched names cascade next backbone automatically.
code: library(taxify)  # Mixed kingdom input: plants, animals, fungi names <- c(   "Quercus robur",         # plant (WFO primary)   "Panthera leo",          # animal (not in WFO, picked up by GBIF)   "Amanita muscaria",      # fungus (not in WFO, picked up by GBIF)   "Salmo trutta",          # fish (not in WFO, picked up by GBIF)   "Arabidopsis thaliana"   # plant (in both WFO and GBIF) )  # WFO first (best for plants), GBIF as fallback (all kingdoms) result <- taxify(names, backend = c("wfo", "gbif"))  # The backend column shows which database matched each name: result[, c("input_name", "backend", "family")] #   input_name            backend family # 1 Quercus robur         wfo     Fagaceae # 2 Panthera leo          gbif    Felidae # 3 Amanita muscaria      gbif    Amanitaceae # 4 Salmo trutta          gbif    Salmonidae # 5 Arabidopsis thaliana  wfo     Brassicaceae  # Enrich with traits: result |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_woodiness()  # Or join custom data: my_traits <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Panthera leo"),   max_height_m = c(35, NA),   body_mass_kg = c(NA, 190) ) result |> add_data(my_traits, species_col = "species")

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: Offline matching. taxify downloads backbone files matches locally. initial download (typically 50–300 MB depending backbone), internet connection needed. Multi-backend. taxify supports seven backbones single function, optional fallback chains cascade unmatched names automatically. Output format. taxify always returns data.frame 16 standardized columns, regardless backend: Speed. taxify uses vectra’s C-level join engine hash indexes genus-blocked fuzzy joins, processing thousands names per second. Reproducibility. taxify pins backbone versions locally records version string backbone_version column every result. backbone file produces output indefinitely. Version pinning also available: taxify_download_vtr("wfo", version = "2024.06") downloads specific release.

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: taxify name matcher. resolves scientific names accepted names, returns classification metadata, joins enrichment layers. Several things taxize packages handle outside scope. Common--scientific name lookup. taxize comm2sci() go “European robin” Erithacus rubecula. taxify matches scientific names, vernacular input. direction, GBIF API (rgbif::name_suggest()) accepts common names returns candidates. Downstream taxa. taxize’s downstream() returned children higher taxon (e.g., species genus). taxify enumerate children. tree-based queries, rotl package provides access Open Tree Life synthetic tree, rgbif’s name_usage() can list children GBIF usage key. Phylogenetic trees. phylogenetic data, use rotl (Open Tree Life) phylomatic. Occurrence data. occurrence data, rgbif spocc standard tools. Sequence data. sequence retrieval, rentrez package handles GenBank/NCBI queries directly. Real-time API lookups. design, taxify queries local files. name added backbone yesterday taxify’s local copy last month, taxify find backbone updated. workflows freshness matters reproducibility, direct API client (rgbif, worrms, ritis) may better fit.

what: When the other packages are the better choice
title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: taxify one tool among several. situations related packages remain right answer: Distribution range queries. rWCVP exposes WCVP’s TDWG-region geography directly wcvp_distribution(), wcvp_occ_mat(), generate_checklist(). taxify covers name-resolution common native-range join add_wcvp(), full geographic queries belong rWCVP. Live API access upstream databases. taxize, rgbif, worrms, ritis, TNRS query backends real time. need name added backbone yesterday, want latest annotation single taxon, packages return immediately. taxify works snapshot disk sees changes backbone updated. Common--scientific lookups. taxize comm2sci() go “European robin” Erithacus rubecula. taxify matches scientific names, vernacular input. direction, rgbif::name_suggest() accepts common names returns candidates. Downstream taxa enumeration. goal list species family subspecies species, taxify provide query. Use rgbif::name_usage(key, data = "children") rotl::tol_subtree(). Wider biodiversity-data cleaning. bdc wraps entire data-cleaning workflow (coordinate cleaning, dataset merging, taxonomic harmonization, occurrence flagging). taxify can replace taxonomic step alone prefer offline backbones taxadb + GNR, rest bdc’s pipeline outside taxify’s scope. Interactive, per-name resolution manual disambiguation. taxize interactive modes user pick among multiple candidates. taxify picks best match automatically (accepted name synonym, species rank higher ranks, lowest ID tiebreaker). manual control ambiguous matches needed, direct API calls may preferable. Column-oriented querying backbone. taxadb stores backbones DuckDB / MonetDB exposes dplyr verbs, natural fit analysis SQL-style transformation backbone. taxify exposes underlying .vtr files vectra kind work, taxadb’s dplyr surface ergonomic custom queries.

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: taxify bundles 12 enrichment datasets cover conservation status, invasive species, functional traits, morphological measurements, vernacular names. joined taxify result piping add_*() functions. enrichment downloads automatically first use cached locally, following pattern backbones. full list: add_conservation_status(), add_invasive_status(), add_wcvp(), add_eive(), add_elton_traits(), add_avonet(), add_pantheria(), add_amphibio(), add_common_names(), add_woodiness(), add_diaz_traits(), add_leda().

title: Migrating from taxize, WorldFlora, and related tools
text: Migrating taxize, WorldFlora, lcvplants, rWCVP, taxadb, Taxonstand taxify means replacing package’s resolution call taxify(backend = ...) optional add_*() enrichment pipes. output flat 16-column data.frame, nested lists long-format join tables, matching runs offline versioned backbone files results change sessions unless user explicitly updates backbone. things taxify handle (distribution queries, downstream taxa, occurrence data, phylogenetic trees, sequence retrieval, live API freshness), specialized packages (rWCVP, rgbif, rotl, spocc, rentrez, worrms, ritis) remain right tools. taxify covers name-matching step comes .

title: Getting started with taxify
text: Almost every biodiversity dataset starts column names. analysis, strings resolve one accepted name per taxon, rarely line : authorship, field qualifiers, capitalization, historical synonyms, hybrids, plain typos keep two records species apart. bare merge() raw strings silently drops every row disagrees, matching come first. taxify() takes character vector returns one standardized table: name cleaned, matched backbone keep disk, synonyms resolved accepted name. Matching runs C vectra engine, web services rate limits, input gives output machine. Thirteen Darwin Core backbones available (WFO, COL, GBIF, ITIS, NCBI, OTT, WoRMS, Euro+Med, Species Fungorum, AlgaeBase, FishBase, SeaLifeBase, Reptile Database), queried offline. first taxify() call downloads backbone (WFO 150 MB) caches taxify_data_dir(). , nothing touches network.
code: library(taxify)

title: Getting started with taxify
text: Hand taxify() vector names. list deliberately small deliberately messy: every entry takes different route accepted name. Ten names, ten rows, every match readable. row also carries genus, authorship, taxon accepted IDs, hybrid flag, backend, exact backbone version (full table wider, shape input). name reaches accepted name different way. animation walks one name time pipeline: clean step strips authorship, qualifier, case; match step exact, case-folded, fuzzy, abbreviated; resolve step follows synonym current name. Quercus robur L. loses authorship matching. cf. Betula pendula loses qualifier. FAGUS SYLVATICA matches case folding (exact_ci). Q. petraea resolves genus initial plus epithet (abbrev). three synonyms (Quercus pedunculata, Pinus abies, Fallopia japonica) resolve accepted names, last current name well-known invader. two typos go fuzzy pass, next thing worth seeing.
code: field_names <- c(   "Quercus robur L.",        # authorship to strip   "Quercus robus",           # typo   "cf. Betula pendula",      # field qualifier   "FAGUS SYLVATICA",         # caps   "Quercus pedunculata",     # historical synonym of Q. robur   "Q. petraea",              # abbreviated genus   "Pinus abies",             # synonym of Picea abies (a different genus)   "Festuca rubrra",          # typo   "Fallopia japonica",       # synonym of Reynoutria japonica (invasive)   "Taraxacum officinale" )  res <- taxify(field_names) res[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "family",         "is_synonym", "match_type", "fuzzy_dist")] #>             input_name        accepted_name       family is_synonym match_type fuzzy_dist #> 1     Quercus robur L.        Quercus robur     Fagaceae      FALSE      exact         NA #> 2        Quercus robus        Quercus robur     Fagaceae      FALSE      fuzzy      0.077 #> 3   cf. Betula pendula       Betula pendula   Betulaceae      FALSE      exact         NA #> 4      FAGUS SYLVATICA      Fagus sylvatica     Fagaceae      FALSE   exact_ci         NA #> 5  Quercus pedunculata        Quercus robur     Fagaceae       TRUE      exact         NA #> 6           Q. petraea      Quercus petraea     Fagaceae      FALSE     abbrev         NA #> 7          Pinus abies          Picea abies     Pinaceae       TRUE      exact         NA #> 8       Festuca rubrra        Festuca rubra      Poaceae      FALSE      fuzzy      0.071 #> 9    Fallopia japonica  Reynoutria japonica Polygonaceae       TRUE      exact         NA #> 10 Taraxacum officinale Taraxacum officinale  Asteraceae      FALSE      exact         NA

what: Stack layers, then test an idea
title: Getting started with taxify
text: Field lists run hundreds names. realistic one, hundred European species, matched one call. Pick layers stack : woodiness, EIVE ecological indicator values, plant height Diaz global trait dataset attach accepted name. species lack EIVE height value, cells NA; R’s statistics functions drop incomplete rows . ordinary analysis. species base-rich soils also sit higher nutrient axis? Across hundred species correlation positive significant, though modest (r = 0.31, p = 0.002): base-rich soils tend carry higher nutrient values. three lines work attribute package can attach.
code: field <- c(   "Quercus petraea", "Pinus sylvestris", "Picea abies", "Betula pendula",   "Acer pseudoplatanus", "Acer platanoides", "Acer campestre", "Corylus avellana",   "Fraxinus excelsior", "Carpinus betulus", "Sorbus aucuparia", "Tilia cordata",   "Ulmus glabra", "Alnus glutinosa", "Salix caprea", "Populus tremula",   "Prunus avium", "Prunus spinosa", "Crataegus monogyna", "Sambucus nigra",   "Cornus sanguinea", "Viburnum opulus", "Euonymus europaeus", "Ligustrum vulgare",   "Frangula alnus", "Juniperus communis", "Taxus baccata", "Larix decidua",   "Abies alba", "Rosa canina", "Rubus idaeus", "Hedera helix",   "Clematis vitalba", "Berberis vulgaris", "Betula pubescens", "Prunus padus",   "Rhamnus cathartica", "Lonicera xylosteum", "Trifolium repens", "Trifolium pratense",   "Festuca ovina", "Dactylis glomerata", "Plantago lanceolata", "Plantago major",   "Plantago media", "Achillea millefolium", "Ranunculus acris", "Ranunculus repens",   "Urtica dioica", "Poa pratensis", "Poa annua", "Galium mollugo",   "Galium aparine", "Bellis perennis", "Cardamine pratensis", "Cirsium arvense",   "Cirsium vulgare", "Daucus carota", "Heracleum sphondylium", "Anthriscus sylvestris",   "Lotus corniculatus", "Medicago lupulina", "Vicia cracca", "Lathyrus pratensis",   "Stellaria media", "Silene dioica", "Silene vulgaris", "Geranium pratense",   "Geranium robertianum", "Glechoma hederacea", "Lamium album", "Prunella vulgaris",   "Ajuga reptans", "Veronica chamaedrys", "Rumex acetosa", "Rumex obtusifolius",   "Chenopodium album", "Capsella bursa-pastoris", "Senecio vulgaris", "Leucanthemum vulgare",   "Centaurea jacea", "Knautia arvensis", "Campanula rotundifolia", "Primula veris",   "Anemone nemorosa", "Filipendula ulmaria", "Lythrum salicaria", "Robinia pseudoacacia",   "Solidago canadensis", "Solidago gigantea", "Impatiens glandulifera",   "Heracleum mantegazzianum", "Prunus serotina", "Quercus rubra",   "Quercus robur L.", "FAGUS SYLVATICA", "Quercus robus",   "cf. Taraxacum officinale", "Quercus pedunculata", "Festuca rubrra" )  dat <- taxify(field) |>   add_woodiness() |>   add_eive() |>   add_diaz_traits()  head(dat[, c("accepted_name", "woodiness", "eive_light",              "eive_reaction", "eive_nutrients", "plant_height_m")], 6) #>         accepted_name woodiness eive_light eive_reaction eive_nutrients plant_height_m #> 1     Quercus petraea     woody       5.83          4.79           3.55          31.44 #> 2    Pinus sylvestris     woody       7.10          5.13           2.69          19.03 #> 3         Picea abies     woody       4.36          4.24           4.31          40.69 #> 4      Betula pendula     woody       6.84          4.38           3.64          12.02 #> 5 Acer pseudoplatanus     woody       3.80          5.92           6.89          24.46 #> 6    Acer platanoides     woody       3.87          6.34           5.66          21.93 cor.test(dat$eive_reaction, dat$eive_nutrients) #>  Pearson's product-moment correlation #> #> data:  dat$eive_reaction and dat$eive_nutrients #> t = 3.2378, df = 96, p-value = 0.001654 #> alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  0.1230070 0.4821711 #> sample estimates: #>       cor #> 0.3137695

title: Constraining matches to a geographic region
text: Fuzzy matching corrects typo finding nearest real name. time nearest name one recorder meant, two species can sit single edit apart living different continents. recorder working Belgium writes slightly misspelled name meant Belgian plant, one-letter neighbour New Zealand. string distance alone tell two apart; geography can. taxify() takes region argument exactly . set , taxify() prefers fuzzy candidates actually occur work sets others aside. never touches exact match, declaring region ever changes spelling correction wins, never name already right.
code: library(taxify)  # a small regional list, with a couple of misspellings to correct field_names <- c(   "Gentiana acaulis", "Primula veris", "Pulsatilla vulgaris",   "Gentiana acaulary", "Primula elatour" )

title: Constraining matches to a geographic region
text: filter rests WCVP, World Checklist Vascular Plants, records accepted species occurs TDWG botanical region. taxify() resolves region input TDWG Level 3 codes, looks candidate fuzzy names WCVP, drops --region candidate better one survives. Three rules keep conservative: filters fuzzy candidates . trusts exact case-folded match given. never drops candidate WCVP range data. Absence data absence region, non-plant match (WCVP record) passes untouched. keeps every candidate name region. filter refines match; refuse one. constraint soft preference. breaks ties toward local species otherwise stays way.

title: Constraining matches to a geographic region
text: clearest input name. bundled WGSRPD crosswalk accepts botanical regions three levels, country, sub-continental region, continent work, case- accent-insensitively. exact match comes back unchanged, since region never touches one. constraint earns keep fuzzy names call: typo two corrections single edit apart one grows Europe, European one wins tie. name conflict resolves exactly without region. "Europe" Level 1 region expands every European code; "Middle Europe" Level 2 region; "Belgium" single Level 3 country. can pass several, union: three-letter token read TDWG code directly, region = "BGM" (Belgium) region = "Belgium" reach place. unrecognised region dropped warning rather failing call, code matches WCVP record simply makes filter -op, typo region degrades gracefully instead producing wrong matches.
code: taxify("Gentiana acaulis", region = "Europe") #>         input_name    accepted_name       family match_type fuzzy_dist backend #> 1 Gentiana acaulis Gentiana acaulis Gentianaceae      exact         NA     WFO taxify(field_names, region = c("Belgium", "Netherlands", "Germany"))

title: Constraining matches to a geographic region
text: data carry coordinates, hand directly. point mapped botanical region point--polygon WGSRPD Level 3 boundaries, resulting codes used way region name . order c(lon, lat). single point, two-column matrix data.frame points, point-geometry spatial object work; sf object terra SpatVector reprojected longitude/latitude way . Points region name can combined, regions union. boundary file downloads stays cached. default lookup runs native ray-casting test, spatial package required. terra sf installed taxify uses instead, faster large point sets, options(taxify.pip_engine = "terra" | "sf" | "native") forces choice.
code: # Brussels: c(longitude, latitude) taxify(field_names, coords = c(4.35, 50.85)) occ <- data.frame(   lon = c(4.35, 5.12, 4.40),   lat = c(50.85, 51.21, 50.50) ) taxify(field_names, coords = occ)

title: Constraining matches to a geographic region
text: default WCVP record counts -region, native introduced alike. range argument narrows . range = "present" default permissive. "native" stricter suits work ignore naturalised populations; species present region introduction satisfy , --region native correction can lose tie. "introduced" mirror image, invasion work wants alien records specifically. argument ignored region set.
code: # only count regions where WCVP lists the species as native taxify(field_names, region = "Europe", range = "native")  # only introduced occurrences taxify(field_names, region = "Europe", range = "introduced")

title: Constraining matches to a geographic region
text: taxify_regions() returns crosswalk can find right code confirm name resolves. argument lists every Level 3 region; search term filters, matching code Level 1, 2, 3 names. crosswalk powers add_wcvp(), codes ones appear native-range enrichment output.
code: taxify_regions("Belgium") #>   code    name  level2_name level1_name #> 1  BGM Belgium Middle Europe      EUROPE # every code Europe expands to nrow(taxify_regions("Europe")) #> [1] 41  # browse the full table head(taxify_regions())

what: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
title: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
text: species names never quite match  Offline taxonomic name matching local Darwin Core backbones, matching done C. Hand column messy species names. taxify cleans , matches backbone already disk, resolves synonyms accepted names, returns one standardized data.frame. Every step runs locally versioned snapshot, API calls, rate limits, input gives output machine. matching engine written C vectra columnar engine.
code: library(taxify)  # match against WFO (downloads the backbone on first use, ~120 MB) taxify(c(   "Quercus robur",   "Pinus abies",        # synonym, resolved to Picea abies   "Quercus robus",      # typo, fuzzy-corrected to Q. robur   "Taraxacum officinale" ))

title: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
text: usual route name resolution, taxize, calls around twenty web services (NCBI, ITIS, GBIF, EOL, IUCN, WoRMS, Tropicos, …). covers everything, ties run network latency, service uptime, rate limits, answer can change runs upstream services update. taxify ships backbones pre-built local snapshots matches C, list thousands resolves seconds result reproducible recorded backbone version. closest local analogue taxadb, also stores backbone snapshots disk; migration vignette walks differences matching strategy, output schema, enrichment.

title: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
text: taxify ships thirteen backbones compressed .vtr files, downloaded matched locally. Pass several form fallback chain: name unmatched first backbone cascades next.
code: # WFO first (plants), then GBIF for whatever WFO doesn't cover taxify(   c("Quercus robur", "Panthera leo", "Amanita muscaria"),   backend = c("wfo", "gbif") )

title: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
text: Input names normalized first, fuzzy pass runs names genuinely differ backbone rather names just carry extra authorship qualifiers: Fuzzy matching configurable (Damerau-Levenshtein, Levenshtein, Jaro-Winkler, distance threshold), runs genus-blocked typo competes names genus. WFO backbone 5,000 plant names (Windows, R 4.5.2), matching local snapshot C avoids per-name cost CSV--RAM approach:
code: "Quercus robur L."            ->  "Quercus robur"      # authorship stripped "Pinus cf. sylvestris"        ->  "Pinus sylvestris"   # qualifier removed "Nothofagus x alpina"         ->  "Nothofagus alpina"  # hybrid marker normalized "Betula pendula (Roth) Doll"  ->  "Betula pendula"     # parenthesized author stripped

title: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
text: taxify() returns one row per input name fixed 16-column schema: matched accepted names, IDs, rank, family, genus, epithet, authorship, synonym hybrid flags, match type (exact, exact_ci, fuzzy, none), fuzzy distance, backend, backbone version used. summary() prints compact digest batch resolved.
code: result <- taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Pinus abies", "Quercus robus", "Taraxacum officinale")) summary(result) #> -- taxify results ---------------------------------------------------- #>   backend: WFO  |  4 names submitted #> #>   matched         4  (exact: 2, case-insensitive: 0, fuzzy: 2) #>   unmatched       0

title: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
text: sixty enrichment layers join published trait status data results backbone-resolved accepted name, synonyms either dataset land key: Sources span kingdoms: IUCN, GRIIS, GBIF common names, WCVP, EIVE, Diaz et al., LEDA, FungalTraits, FUNGuild, AlgaeTraits, EltonTraits, AVONET, PanTHERIA, AmphiBIO, FISHMORPH, FishBase, AnAge, GloNAF, LepTraits, AnimalTraits, regional plant-trait sets France (Baseflor), Britain (Ecoflora), Germany (FloraWeb), . enrichments vignette lists full set references licenses. join table, add_data() auto-detects species column, matches backbone(s) used original call, left-joins. accepts data.frames, CSV, CSV.GZ, XLSX, SQLite, .vtr.
code: # plants taxify(plant_names) |>   add_conservation_status() |>   # IUCN Red List   add_invasive_status("AT") |>   # GRIIS   add_woodiness() |>             # Zanne et al.   add_eive()                     # EIVE indicator values  # fish taxify(fish_names, backend = "col") |>   add_fishbase() |>              # FishBase morphology & ecology   add_fish_traits()              # FISHMORPH functional traits result |> add_data("TRY_traits.csv") result |> add_data("TRY_traits.csv", cols = c("LeafArea", "SLA", "PlantHeight"))

what: Check a list before you trust it
title: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots
text: inspect() returns names look wrong, labelled stands name use instead. catches typos, retired synonyms, made-genera, near-duplicate spellings, lone animal list plants, ranks whether need decision, second look, just optional cleanup. regional field lists, region steers fuzzy correction toward species actually occur work, misspelling resolves plant grows even one-letter neighbour another continent sits just close spelling. Pass region name, TDWG code, coordinates.
code: inspect(field_names)                  # offline register and list checks inspect(field_names, backbones = TRUE) # also typos, synonyms, ambiguity taxify(field_names, region = "Belgium") taxify(field_names, coords = c(4.35, 50.85))

title: Add alien species first record years — add_alien_first_records
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). country Character. ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code(s), "". Single code (e.g., ""): adds columns without suffix. Multiple codes (e.g., c("", "DE")): adds columns country suffix (e.g., alien_first_record_AT). "": adds one column set per country dataset. verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add alien species first record years — add_alien_first_records

what: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
title: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
text: Joins Baseflor (Julve, Programme Catminat) plant traits taxify() result looking accepted_name. Baseflor covers vascular flora France neighbouring regions, providing flowering phenology, pollination breeding biology, dispersal mode, floral fruit morphology.

title: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
code: add_baseflor(x, verbose = TRUE)

title: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
text: data.frame additional columns: flower_begin_month First month flowering (1-12). flower_end_month Last month flowering (1-12). value smaller flower_begin_month denotes flowering period wraps across new year (e.g. begin 10, end 6). pollination_vector Pollination vector(s): insect, wind, water, self, apogamy. Comma-separated one applies. dispersal_mode Diaspore dispersal mode(s): anemochory, barochory, epizoochory, endozoochory, myrmecochory, hydrochory, autochory, dyszoochory. Comma-separated one applies. breeding_system Sexual system: hermaphroditic, monoecious, dioecious, gynodioecious, androdioecious, gynomonoecious, polygamous. flower_colour Flower colour(s): white, yellow, pink, green, blue, brown, black. Comma-separated one applies. fruit_type Fruit type: achene, capsule, caryopsis, drupe, legume, silique, berry, follicle, cone, samara, pyxid. woody_growth_form Woody growth form woody taxa: tree, small tree, large tree, shrub, bush, subshrub, liana, parasite. NA non-woody (herbaceous) taxa. continentality Ellenberg-style continentality indicator value (1-9), axis absent EIVE. salinity Ellenberg-style salinity indicator value (0-9), axis absent EIVE.

title: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
text: Source: Baseflor, Programme Catminat (Julve 1998 ff.). Coverage: ~7,000 vascular plant taxa France neighbouring regions. Data released ODbL 1.0 / CC -SA 2.0. ecological indicator values light, temperature, moisture, reaction, nutrient axes, see add_eive() (European calibration). Raunkiaer life form seed, leaf, clonality traits Northwest European flora, see add_leda().

title: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
text: Julve, Ph. (1998 ff.) baseflor. Index botanique, ecologique et chorologique de la Flore de France. Programme Catminat.

title: Add plant traits from Baseflor (Catminat / Julve) — add_baseflor
code: # Runs offline against the bundled example database. old <- options(taxify.data_dir = taxify_example_data())  taxify("Bellis perennis") |>   add_baseflor()  options(old)

what: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
text: Joins external data source (CSV file data.frame) taxify() result. Species names external data matched backbone(s) used original taxify() call, join performed accepted_id — synonyms either dataset resolve key.

title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
code: add_data(   x,   data,   species_col = NULL,   table = NULL,   sheet = NULL,   start_row = NULL,   cols = NULL,   group_col = NULL,   groups = "all",   fuzzy = TRUE,   fuzzy_threshold = 0.2,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). data One : data.frame already R. file path .csv, .csv.gz, .tsv, .tsv.gz, .xlsx, .sqlite/.db, .vtr file (read via vectra). species_col Character. Name column data contains species names. NULL (default), auto-detected matching head(10) character column backbone. table Character. Required data SQLite file — table name read. sheet Integer character. Sheet read data .xlsx file. Default NULL (auto-detect sheet containing species names). Set explicitly skip auto-detection. start_row Integer. Row column headers begin .xlsx file. Default NULL (auto-detect scanning first 20 rows header row produces species name matches). Set explicitly layout known. cols Character vector column names data join. NULL (default), columns except species_col joined. group_col Character NULL. Column data defines groups (e.g., country codes, regions). set, output pivoted wide format one column per group (e.g., trait_AT, trait_DE), just like built-grouped enrichments. Use taxify_long() reshape back long format. Default NULL (flat join, one row per species). groups Character vector "". groups include group_col set. Default "". fuzzy Logical. Enable fuzzy matching names data. Default TRUE. fuzzy_threshold Numeric. Maximum allowed distance fuzzy matches. Default 0.2. verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
text: input data.frame additional columns data, joined via backbone-resolved accepted_id. Columns data collide existing columns x prefixed "data_".

title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
text: workflow: Read data (CSV data.frame). Identify species column (explicit auto-detected). Match species names backbone(s) original taxify() call, obtaining accepted_id row. Check conflicting duplicates: multiple rows data resolve accepted_id different values, error raised (unless group_col set). Exact duplicates produce warning deduplicated. Left-join accepted_id.

title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
text: data multiple rows per species (e.g., one row per species per country), set group_col produce wide output suffixed columns. format built-grouped enrichments.

title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
text: species_col specified, add_data() takes first 10 rows character column runs taxify(). column highest match rate selected. column achieves least 50% matches, error raised asking user specify species_col explicitly.

title: Add custom data by taxonomic matching — add_data
code: # Runs offline against the bundled example database. old <- options(taxify.data_dir = taxify_example_data())  result <- taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris")) traits <- data.frame(species = c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris"),                      height = c(30, 25)) result |> add_data(traits, species_col = "species")  options(old)

what: Add British plant traits from Ecoflora — add_ecoflora
title: Add British plant traits from Ecoflora — add_ecoflora
text: Joins traits Ecological Flora British Isles (Fitter & Peat 1994) taxify() result looking accepted_name. Ecoflora covers vascular flora British Isles, providing canopy height, leaf traits, life form, flowering phenology, pollination reproduction, seed weight, British-calibrated Ellenberg indicator values. Every column carries _uk suffix mark British-flora calibration avoid collisions chained plant-trait enrichments (e.g. add_baseflor() France, add_floraweb() Germany).

text: data.frame additional _uk columns: height_max_mm_uk, height_min_mm_uk Canopy height range (mm). leaf_area_uk Leaf area class. leaf_longevity_uk Leaf longevity (e.g. evergreen, deciduous). root_system_uk Root system type. photosynthetic_pathway_uk Photosynthetic pathway (C3/C4/CAM). life_form_uk Raunkiaer life form. reproduction_uk Reproduction method. flower_begin_month_uk, flower_end_month_uk Flowering months (1-12). pollination_vector_uk Pollen vector(s). seed_weight_mg_uk Seed weight (mg). propagule_uk Propagule / dispersule type. ell_light_uk, ell_moisture_uk, ell_reaction_uk, ell_nitrogen_uk, ell_salt_uk Ellenberg indicator values calibrated British flora (light, moisture, reaction, nitrogen, salt).

title: Add British plant traits from Ecoflora — add_ecoflora
text: Source: Ecoflora (Ecological Flora British Isles). Ecoflora bulk download API; bundled dataset collected one species time redistributed source licence (CC -NC-SA 4.0). .vtr downloaded taxify release first use cached. French-flora traits see add_baseflor(); German-flora traits see add_floraweb(); European-calibration indicator values see add_eive().

what: Add German plant traits from FloraWeb — add_floraweb
title: Add German plant traits from FloraWeb — add_floraweb
text: Joins traits FloraWeb (Bundesamt fuer Naturschutz) taxify() result looking accepted_name. FloraWeb live national portal carrying BiolFlor trait data (Klotz, Kuehn & Durka 2002) together Rothmaler morphology Ellenberg indicator values. enrichment covers full per-species trait profile scraped four FloraWeb trait pages: morphology, reproductive biology, nine Ellenberg indicator values, ploidy chromosome number, chorological distribution. Every column carries _de suffix mark German-flora calibration avoid collisions chained plant-trait enrichments (e.g. add_ecoflora() Britain, add_baseflor() France).

title: Add German plant traits from FloraWeb — add_floraweb
code: add_floraweb(x, verbose = TRUE)

title: Add German plant traits from FloraWeb — add_floraweb
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add German plant traits from FloraWeb — add_floraweb
text: data.frame German trait columns (suffixed _de), grouped : Morphology height_de, life_form_de, leaf_shape_de, leaf_anatomy_de, leaf_persistence_de, storage_organs_de, flowering_months_de, flowering_months_biolflor_de, flowering_phase_de, phenological_season_de, description_de. Reproductive biology pollination_vector_de, pollinator_de, pollinator_reward_de, flower_type_de, flower_class_de, dispersal_type_de, diaspore_type_de, germinule_type_de, reproduction_type_de, vegetative_spread_de, fertilization_type_de, apomixis_de, dicliny_de, dichogamy_de, self_incompatibility_de, si_mechanism_de, ploidy_de, chromosome_number_de, chromosome_freq_de, chromosomes_de. Ecology nine Ellenberg indicator values ell_light_de, ell_temperature_de, ell_continentality_de, ell_moisture_de, ell_moisture_variability_de, ell_reaction_de, ell_nitrogen_de, ell_salt_de, heavy_metal_resistance_de, plus strategy_type_de (Grime CSR), habitat_site_de, formation_de, plant_community_de, biotope_type_de, forest_binding_de, hemeroby_de, urbanity_de. Distribution floristic_zones_de, areal_formula_de, areal_type_de, oceanity_de, range_centre_de, world_range_size_de, world_range_frequency_de, world_range_position_de, world_range_hazard_de, germany_range_share_de, germany_responsibility_de. Categorical traits several applicable values joined "; ". Trait values German (published FloraWeb / BiolFlor).

title: Add German plant traits from FloraWeb — add_floraweb
text: Source: FloraWeb (https://www.floraweb.de/), Bundesamt fuer Naturschutz, Bonn. FloraWeb bulk export API; bundled dataset scraped per species (accessed 2026-06-24) access date dataset version. trait data largely derive BiolFlor, per BioFresh metadata statement publicly available may used without restrictions provided acknowledged cited correctly. .vtr downloaded taxify release first use cached. British-flora traits see add_ecoflora(); French-flora traits see add_baseflor(); European-calibration indicator values see add_eive().

title: Add German plant traits from FloraWeb — add_floraweb

title: Add fungal lifestyle and trait data (FungalTraits) — add_fungal_traits
text: data.frame additional columns: primary_lifestyle Primary ecological role (e.g., saprotroph, mycorrhizal, pathogen, endophyte, lichenized, parasite). secondary_lifestyle Secondary ecological role, . growth_form Morphological growth form (e.g., agaricoid, corticioid, polyporoid, yeast). fruitbody_type Fruiting body morphology (e.g., gasteroid, pileate, resupinate). decay_substrate Substrate type saprotrophic genera (e.g., wood, litter, dung, soil). plant_pathogenic_capacity Capacity cause plant disease (e.g., high, medium, low, none). animal_biotrophic_capacity Capacity animal biotrophy. endophytic_interaction_capability Capacity endophytic interactions plants. ectomycorrhiza_exploration_type Exploration type ectomycorrhizal genera (e.g., contact, short, medium, long).

what: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild
title: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild
text: Joins FUNGuild trophic mode, guild, growth morphology, confidence data taxify() result looking accepted_name. Species-level matches take priority; genus-level guild assignments used fallback unmatched species.

title: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild
code: add_funguild(x, verbose = TRUE)

title: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild
text: data.frame additional columns: trophic_mode Trophic mode (e.g., Pathotroph, Saprotroph, Symbiotroph, hyphenated combinations). guild Functional guild (e.g., "Ectomycorrhizal", "Plant Pathogen", "Wood Saprotroph"). funguild_growth_form Growth morphology (e.g., "Agaricoid", "Microfungus"). Prefixed avoid collision FungalTraits. confidence_ranking Confidence guild assignment (Possible, Probable, Highly Probable).

title: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild
text: Source: FUNGuild (Nguyen et al. 2016, CC 4.0). Coverage: ~13k taxa. Fungi . enrichment first attempts species-level matching. species without direct match, falls back genus-level guild assignments FUNGuild's genus-rank entries.

title: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild

title: Add fungal functional guild data (FUNGuild) — add_funguild

title: Add naturalized alien flora status (GloNAF) — add_glonaf

Units follow GRooT data paper; see reference .

what: Add plant traits from LEDA Traitbase — add_leda
title: Add plant traits from LEDA Traitbase — add_leda
text: Joins LEDA Traitbase (Kleyer et al. 2008) plant functional traits taxify() result looking accepted_name. LEDA provides species-level trait data NW European plant species, covering life form, dispersal, seed, leaf, clonality traits.

title: Add plant traits from LEDA Traitbase — add_leda
code: add_leda(x, verbose = TRUE)

title: Add plant traits from LEDA Traitbase — add_leda
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add plant traits from LEDA Traitbase — add_leda
text: data.frame additional columns: raunkiaer_life_form Primary Raunkiaer life form classification (phanerophyte, chamaephyte, hemicryptophyte, geophyte, therophyte, helophyte, hydrophyte). raunkiaer_variable 1 species assigned multiple Raunkiaer forms, 0 otherwise. dispersal_type Primary dispersal type (anemochory, zoochory, hydrochory, autochory, barochory, dysochory). terminal_velocity_ms Seed terminal velocity m/s (species median). seed_mass_mg Seed mass mg (species median). Prefixed leda_ .vtr avoid collision Diaz traits. canopy_height_m Canopy height meters (species median). leaf_mass_mg Leaf dry mass mg (species median). sla_mm2_mg Specific leaf area mm\(^2\)/mg (species median). clonal_growth Capable clonal growth (1 = yes, 0 = ). buoyancy Seed buoyancy classification.

title: Add plant traits from LEDA Traitbase — add_leda
text: Source: LEDA Traitbase (Kleyer et al. 2008). Coverage: ~8,000 NW European plant species. Raunkiaer life form bud-position classification system: phanerophyte = buds >25 cm soil, chamaephyte = buds near soil surface, hemicryptophyte = buds soil surface, geophyte (cryptophyte) = buds soil, therophyte = annual survives seed.

title: Add plant traits from LEDA Traitbase — add_leda
text: Kleyer M et al. (2008) LEDA Traitbase: database life-history traits Northwest European flora. Journal Ecology 96:1266-1274.

code: # Runs offline against the bundled example database. old <- options(taxify.data_dir = taxify_example_data())  taxify("Arrhenatherum elatius") |>   add_leda()  options(old)

title: Add Italian plant traits from Pignatti (on demand, via TR8) — add_pignatti
text: Fetches Italian Ellenberg-type indicator values, life form, chorotype Pignatti's Flora d'Italia (Pignatti, Menegoni & Pietrosanti 2005) species taxify() result, using TR8 package, joins accepted_name. TR8 ships values bundled, works offline.

code: add_pignatti(x, verbose = TRUE)

title: Add Italian plant traits from Pignatti (on demand, via TR8) — add_pignatti
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add Italian plant traits from Pignatti (on demand, via TR8) — add_pignatti
text: data.frame additional columns: light_it, temperature_it, continentality_it, moisture_it, reaction_it, nutrients_it, salinity_it Ellenberg-type indicator values calibrated Italian flora (codes; X = indifferent, 0 = applicable). life_form_it Life form Italian flora. chorotype_it Chorological type (distribution).

title: Add Italian plant traits from Pignatti (on demand, via TR8) — add_pignatti
text: values originate copyrighted publication, taxify redistribute . function reads copy bundled suggested package TR8 (redistributes TR8's GPL, attribution); taxify ships none internet access required. European-calibration indicator values see add_eive().

title: Add Italian plant traits from Pignatti (on demand, via TR8) — add_pignatti

title: Add Italian plant traits from Pignatti (on demand, via TR8) — add_pignatti
code: old <- options(taxify.data_dir = taxify_example_data())  # \donttest{ # add_pignatti() fetches Italian trait data on demand via the TR8 package. taxify("Abies alba") |>   add_pignatti() # }  options(old)

what: Add reptile ecological traits and distribution (ReptTraits) — add_repttraits
title: Add reptile ecological traits and distribution (ReptTraits) — add_repttraits
text: Joins species-level reptile traits ReptTraits (Oskyrko et al. 2024) taxify() result looking accepted_name. ReptTraits built Reptile Database taxonomy, joins cleanly reptiledb backbone (backbone resolves Reptile Database accepted names).

title: Add reptile ecological traits and distribution (ReptTraits) — add_repttraits
code: add_repttraits(x, verbose = TRUE)

title: Add reptile ecological traits and distribution (ReptTraits) — add_repttraits
text: x data.frame returned taxify(). verbose Logical. Default TRUE.

title: Add reptile ecological traits and distribution (ReptTraits) — add_repttraits
text: data.frame additional columns: biogeographic_realm Main biogeographic realm (e.g. Neotropic, Palearctic, Afrotropic, Australo-Pacific, Marine). microhabitat Microhabitat (e.g. Terrestrial, Saxicolous, Arboreal). habitat_type Habitat type(s) (e.g. Forest, Desert, Wetlands). elevation_min_m Minimum recorded elevation metres. elevation_max_m Maximum recorded elevation metres. mean_annual_temp_c Mean annual temperature across range (degrees Celsius). insular_endemic Whether species insular/endemic ("Yes"/""). body_mass_g Maximum body mass grams. svl_mm Maximum snout-vent length (straight carapace length turtles) mm. total_length_mm Maximum total length mm. longevity_yr Maximum longevity years. diet Diet category (e.g. Carnivorous, Herbivorous, Omnivorous). reproductive_mode Reproductive mode (oviparous/viviparous/...). clutch_size Mean clutch litter size. active_time Activity time (Diurnal/Nocturnal/Cathemeral). foraging_mode Foraging mode (ACT active / AMB ambush / Mixed).

title: Add reptile ecological traits and distribution (ReptTraits) — add_repttraits
text: layer carries per-species distribution signal – biogeographic realm, elevation range mean climate – alongside body-size life-history traits, across reptiles (snakes, lizards, amphisbaenians, turtles, crocodiles tuatara), lizards . Source: ReptTraits v1.2 (Oskyrko et al. 2024, Scientific Data, CC 4.0). Coverage: 12,060 reptile species. biogeographic realm climate fields give coarse, realm-level range signal; fine-grained (TDWG-level) range like plant ranges used region constraint.

title: Add reptile ecological traits and distribution (ReptTraits) — add_repttraits
text: Oskyrko O, Mi C, Meiri S, Du W (2024) ReptTraits: comprehensive dataset ecological traits reptiles. Scientific Data 11:243. doi:10.1038/s41597-024-03079-5

what: Inspect a name list for probable typos and other anomalies — inspect
title: Inspect a name list for probable typos and other anomalies — inspect
text: quality-control pass name list. default inspect() match names backbones: plain character vector runs checks need matching – genus register rest batch – fast offline. also surface match-based anomalies (typo, synonym, ambiguous, geographic), either set backbones = TRUE (matches every installed backbone, listed report) match first inspect result (taxify(x) |> inspect()). Either way returns rows look anomalous, labelled stands , known, name use instead.

title: Inspect a name list for probable typos and other anomalies — inspect
code: inspect(   x,   backbones = FALSE,   region = NULL,   coords = NULL,   range = c("present", "native", "introduced"),   min_tier = c("note", "review", "unresolved"),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Inspect a name list for probable typos and other anomalies — inspect
text: x character vector names, taxify_result taxify(). backbones Logical. x character vector, TRUE matches every installed backbone (via taxify()) match-based labels available; FALSE (default) runs register list checks , matching. backbones used printed report header. Ignored x already taxify_result (matched already). region, coords, range Geographic constraint geographic / out_of_range checks, taxify(). act taxify_result (carries accepted names need); character vector nothing matched place, effect. min_tier Lowest tier report: "note" (default, everything), "review", "unresolved". verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default TRUE.

title: Inspect a name list for probable typos and other anomalies — inspect
text: taxify_inspection data.frame (one row per anomalous name, ordered -notable first) columns input_name, suggestion (name use instead, NA), anomalies (|-joined labels), tier (ordered factor note < review < unresolved), reason, fuzzy_dist, backend. Zero rows means nothing stood .

title: Inspect a name list for probable typos and other anomalies — inspect
text: Checks need matching (run character vector result): unknown genus genus register – union 13 backbones' genera – backbone recognises . strong "probably real name" signal. near_duplicate near-twin frequent name list (small edit distance), probably misspelling . Computed list alone, catches typos names backbone contains. outlier_group name's kingdom group (register) tiny minority otherwise group-coherent list – lone animal fungus among plants, typically cross-kingdom homonym typo. Checks read taxify() result (present inspect one): typo Resolved fuzzy correction (match_type = "fuzzy"): input likely contains spelling error; suggestion name. ambiguous homonym resolving one accepted taxon. geographic matched species real WCVP record declared region / coords (vascular plants ). out_of_range region declared, yet matched species' range falls outside list's main TDWG continents (skipped globally spread lists). case Resolved ignoring case (match_type = "exact_ci"). synonym input outdated synonym; suggestion current accepted name. Rows anomaly dropped. row gets tier describing needs, bad : unresolved (usable name – act ), review (name identity uncertain – verify ), note (correct, optional cleanup). unknown unresolved; identity-uncertain labels review; case synonym note. anomaly may intended, tier triage hint, verdict. list-context labels (near_duplicate, out_of_range, outlier_group) judge name rest batch, apply single name: inspect() one name warns reports per-name labels. register checks (unknown, register-derived outlier_group) need genus register installed; without skipped (message verbose).

title: Inspect a name list for probable typos and other anomalies — inspect
code: old <- options(taxify.data_dir = taxify_example_data())  # On raw names: register + list checks (no matching) inspect(c("Quercus robur", "Bogusus fakus", "Carexus mysteriosa",           "Carexus mysteriosa", "Carexus mysteryosa"))  # Opt in to matching to also get typos, synonyms, ambiguity inspect(c("Quercus robur", "Quercus robus"), backbones = TRUE)  # Or match yourself and inspect the result taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Quercus robus")) |> inspect()  options(old)

what: Normalize aggregate markers on canonical names (build-time) — normalize_aggregate_name
title: Normalize aggregate markers on canonical names (build-time) — normalize_aggregate_name
text: Folds every aggregate marker backbone enrichment source may use one canonical form, "<binomial> aggr.", taxify's matching engine enrichment join recognize aggregates uniformly regardless source spelling. Two cases handled: name already carrying marker (agg., aggr., -agg, s.l., sensu lato, coll. sp.) rewritten "<binomial> aggr."; name aggregate rank (taxon_rank "SPECIES AGGREGATE", "AGGR.", "COLL. SP.") carries marker gets " aggr." appended. Exported taxifydb build pipeline build runtime sides share one definition.

title: Vectorized Latin orthographic normalization — normalize_epithets
text: Pipeline: Lowercase. Strip Latin-1 diacritics ligatures (e-acute e, ae-ligature ae, sharp-s ss, etc.), applied genus epithet. Orthographic alternation epithet : ae/oe -> , trailing ii -> , y -> , ph -> f, rh -> r, th -> t. Step 2 runs step 3, ae-ligature -> ae -> oe-ligature -> oe -> fold key de-ligatured forms.
